coredata.dc:title: The future of plant based green carbon dots as cancer Nanomedicine: From current progress to future Perspectives and beyond

coredata.dc:description:
Background The emergence of carbon dots (CDs) as anticancer agents had sparked a transformation in cancer research and treatment strategies. These fluorescent CDs, initially introduced in the early 2000 s, possess exceptional biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence, and surface modification capabilities, positioning them as promising tools in biomedical applications.
Aim of Review The review encapsulates the transformative trajectory of green CDs as future anticancer nanomedicine, poised to redefine the strategies employed in the ongoing fight against cancer.
Key Scientific Concepts of Review The versatility of CDs was rooted in their various synthesis approaches and sustainable strategies, enabling their adaptability for diverse therapeutic uses. In vitro studies had showcased CDs' selective cytotoxicity against cancer cells while sparing healthy counterparts, forming the basis for targeted therapeutic potential. This selectivity had been attributed to the reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, which opened avenues for targeted interventions. The role of CDs in combination therapies, synergizing with chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and targeted approaches was then investigated to heighten their anticancer efficacy. Notably, in vivo studies highlight CDs' remarkable biocompatibility and minimal side effects, endorsing their translational promise. Integration with conventional cancer treatments such as chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and immunotherapy amplified the versatility and effectiveness of CDs. The exploration of CDs' applications in photo-induced treatments further solidified their significance, positioning them as photosensitizers (PS) in photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal agents (PA) in photothermal therapy (PTT). In PDT, CDs triggered the generation of ROS upon light exposure, facilitating cancer cell elimination, while in PTT, they induced localized hyperthermia within cancer cells, enhancing therapeutic outcomes. In vitro and in vivo investigations validated CDs' efficacy in PDT and PTT, affirming their potential for integration into combination therapies. Looking ahead, the future of CDs in anticancer treatment encompasses bioavailability, biocompatibility, synergistic treatments, tumor targeting, artificial intelligence (AI) and robotics integration, personalized medicine, and clinical translation. This transformative odyssey of CDs as future anticancer agents is poised to redefine the paradigm of cancer treatment strategies.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The emergence of carbon dots (CDs) as anticancer agents had sparked a transformation in cancer research and treatment strategies. These fluorescent CDs, initially introduced in the early 2000 s, possess exceptional biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence, and surface modification capabilities, positioning them as promising tools in biomedical applications.
The review encapsulates the transformative trajectory of green CDs as future anticancer nanomedicine, poised to redefine the strategies employed in the ongoing fight against cancer.

The versatility of CDs was rooted in their various synthesis approaches and sustainable strategies, enabling their adaptability for diverse therapeutic uses. In vitro studies had showcased CDs' selective cytotoxicity against cancer cells while sparing healthy counterparts, forming the basis for targeted therapeutic potential. This selectivity had been attributed to the reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, which opened avenues for targeted interventions. The role of CDs in combination therapies, synergizing with chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and targeted approaches was then investigated to heighten their anticancer efficacy. Notably, in vivo studies highlight CDs' remarkable biocompatibility and minimal side effects, endorsing their translational promise. Integration with conventional cancer treatments such as chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and immunotherapy amplified the versatility and effectiveness of CDs. The exploration of CDs' applications in photo-induced treatments further solidified their significance, positioning them as photosensitizers (PS) in photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal agents (PA) in photothermal therapy (PTT). In PDT, CDs triggered the generation of ROS upon light exposure, facilitating cancer cell elimination, while in PTT, they induced localized hyperthermia within cancer cells, enhancing therapeutic outcomes. In vitro and in vivo investigations validated CDs' efficacy in PDT and PTT, affirming their potential for integration into combination therapies. Looking ahead, the future of CDs in anticancer treatment encompasses bioavailability, biocompatibility, synergistic treatments, tumor targeting, artificial intelligence (AI) and robotics integration, personalized medicine, and clinical translation. This transformative odyssey of CDs as future anticancer agents is poised to redefine the paradigm of cancer treatment strategies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Cancer poses an enduring global health challenge, with its pervasive incidence and formidable mortality underscoring the need for innovative therapeutic approaches. Despite notable advancement in conventional cancer treatments, including surgical interventions, chemotherapy, and radiation therapy, had advanced significantly, they often come at the cost of undesirable side effects, incomplete tumor eradication, and the potential for tumor recurrence [1]. The insufficiencies of conventional treatments have paved the way for novel therapeutic strategies, and the rise of nanotechnology has offered a potentially transformative avenue in cancer treatment. Among the diverse array of nanoparticles that have captured scientific attention, CDs have emerged as a promising candidate poised to revolutionize the landscape of anticancer therapy.

The journey towards using nanoparticles in cancer therapy is grounded in the quest for more effective and targeted treatment approaches. Conventional therapies, although lifesaving, often display limitations. Chemotherapy, for instance, not only affects cancer cells but also damages healthy tissues, leading to severe side effects such as hair loss, nausea, vomiting and compromised immunity [2]. Radiation therapy, while localized, can also have detrimental effects on surrounding tissues [3]. The need for a more refined and precision-oriented approach has thus stimulated the exploration of nanoparticles, capable of specifically targeting cancer cells while sparing healthy ones. CDs, with their unique physicochemical properties and versatile functionalities, have emerged as a promising contender in this endeavour.

The historical trajectory of CDs is traced back to their inception as fluorescent carbon nanoparticles in the early 2000 s when fluorescent carbon nanoparticles were first synthesized [4]. Since then, these nanoparticles have been systematically studied and characterized, revealing a range of attributes that make them exceptionally well suited for a plethora of biomedical applications. Their exceptional properties such as excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, tunable fluorescence, and ease of surface modification capabilities, etc. have set the stage for their exploration in diverse therapeutic contexts [5]. Synthesis approaches, ranging from chemical methods to green and eco-friendly strategies, underscore the versatility of CDs, accommodating the demands of various applications, including cancer therapy.

The foundation of CDs' potential in cancer therapy rests on cytotoxicity studies. An amalgamation of in vitro and in vivo experiments showcases the selective effectiveness of CDs in targeting specific cancer cell lines while exhibiting minimal cytotoxicity towards healthy normal cells [6,7]. This selectivity forms the foundation for their potential as targeted anticancer agents. The exceptional biocompatibility of CDs opens avenues to synergistic combinations with established treatment modalities, transforming them into potent collaborators in the fight against cancer. The mechanisms underlying this cytotoxicity have been linked to the production of ROS, offering new routes for targeted interventions [7–9]. The versatility of CDs is further underscored by their role in combination therapies. Reports of CDs' compatibility with chemotherapy, targeted therapy, radiotherapy, and immunotherapy highlight their role in augmenting the therapeutic outcomes of these established treatments [10–14]. Moreover, their excellent biocompatibility and minimal side effects, as evidenced in in vivo studies, underscore their translational potential. These findings pave the way for the exploration of CDs as complements to conventional treatments, amplifying their therapeutic impact.

The spotlight then turns to the utilization of CDs in photo-induced cancer treatments, specifically PDT and PTT elucidated through meticulous in vitro and in vivo studies. In the former, CDs function as PS, generating ROS upon light irradiation to eliminate cancer cells [15]. In the latter, CDs serve as PA, inducing localized hyperthermia within cancer cells when exposed to light [15]. This multifunctionality positions CDs at the forefront of a therapeutic revolution, wherein their light-induced properties hold the promise of precise and controlled tumor eradication. Furthermore, the synergistic potential of CDs comes to the fore as they seamlessly integrate into combination therapies. The integration of CDs with chemotherapy, immunotherapy and photo-induced therapy showcases their ability to enhance the effectiveness of these treatments through various mechanisms [16–19]. This dual-pronged approach illustrates the multifaceted nature of CDs in orchestrating comprehensive anticancer strategies.

As the review navigates through the landscape of CDs' applications, it concludes by contemplating the future of these nanomaterials in anticancer treatment. The potential impact of CDs spans a spectrum of aspects, including their bioavailability, biocompatibility, role in synergistic treatments, tumor targeting capabilities, integration with AI and robotics, contributions to personalized medicine, and their journey towards clinical translation.

In essence, the review encapsulates the transformative trajectory of CDs as future anticancer agents, poised to redefine the strategies employed in the ongoing fight against cancer. From the historical evolution to the intricate cytotoxicity mechanism, from the integration with established therapies to their role in photo-induced treatments, and finally to their transformative potential in the future of cancer treatment, every aspect is meticulously explored. The promise of CDs as a beacon of hope in the fight against cancer shines brightly, paving the way for an era of precision, synergy, and innovation in anticancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon Dots: Overview of History, Synthesis, characterization and challenges
ce:section.ce:para: CDs encompassing graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are a fascinating class of zero-dimensional fluorescence nanoparticles that have garnered substantial attention in recent years due to their remarkable attributes and diverse applications, including biomedicine, electronics, optoelectronics, and sensing. They are nanoscale carbon-based materials with diameters typically ranging from 1 nm to 10 nm, that exhibited unique photophysical properties [5,20]. The journey of CDs can be traced back to the early 2000 s when Xu et al. [4] first observed fluorescent carbon nanoparticles during the purification and separation of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs). These nanosized carbon particles exhibited unique fluorescence properties, differentiating them from other carbon materials. The term “carbon dots” was coined in 2006 by Sun et al. [21] who successfully disclosed a synthetic track to develop CQDs with significantly enhanced fluorescence emission [22]. Since then, numerous studies have been conducted to explore the methods of synthesizing, their properties, and the potential applications of CDs. The field of CDs research has witnessed significant growth and attracted the attention of scientists from various disciplines.

The discovery of CDs has opened a new frontier in nanomaterials. Initially, they were primarily investigated for their intriguing optical properties, particularly their fluorescence behaviour. CDs are akin to inorganic fluorescent semiconductor nanoparticles, boasting robust and tunable fluorescence emission [23,24]. Depending on the surface chemistry and synthesis method, CDs can exhibit fluorescence across a broad range of wavelengths, from ultraviolet (UV) to near-infrared (NIR) [25]. However, as research progressed, the unique characteristics of CDs, such as their low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, small size, tunable photoluminescence, electronic and versatile surface chemistry, sparked interest in their application in fields like disease detection, PS, photocatalysis, electrochemical luminescence and optoelectronics [5,24,26]. For instance, (1) CDs find utility in visualizing biological system both in vitro and in vivo due to their excellent biocompatibility and minimal toxicity behaviour [5,27]; (2) CDs can be easily conjugated with targeting ligands or therapeutic agents, allowing for targeted drug delivery and imaging of specific cell types or tissues due to their small size and surface functionalization potential [28]; (3) CDs with intriguing electronic characteristics can be served as effective electron donors or acceptors, make them viable candidates for catalytic applications, including the catalytic degradation of organic compounds in aqueous environments, catalysis of hydrogen production by splitting water and catalysis of the oxygen reduction reactions (ORRs) in fuel cells [29]; (4) CDs also can be used for the binding and detection of various molecules, due to their useful physiochemical properties including abundant surface functional groups, large surface areas, excellent water dispersibility [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis routes for carbon dots and their respective limitations
ce:section.ce:para: In the past decade, CDs synthesis has been a subject of intense research, offering a wide array of applications. To date, the synthesis of CDs encompassed a variety of approaches which can be broadly categorized into two main manners, namely “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods, each with distinct characteristics [5]. The choice of the synthesis method has a significant impact on the properties of the resulting CDs. In the “top-down” approach, large carbon precursor molecules such as carbon soot, coal, graphite, activated carbon, and carbon nanotubes are broken into nanoscale carbon particles by methods like chemical oxidation, electrochemical oxidation, laser ablation and arc discharge [31]. Such methods can produce monodispersed CDs with precisely defined molecular structure and size. Yet, they require expensive materials and setup, harsh and tedious reaction times and conditions and usually have low production yield [31,32]. In addition, the obtained CDs are prone to π-π stacking due to the lack of functional groups [31]. Therefore, “top-down” approaches for CDs synthesis are less commonly adopted compared to “bottom-up” methods nowadays. On the other hand, “bottom-up” methods involve the transformation of CDs from suitable organic molecules under the action of the “oxidized decomposition – crosslinking – carbonization” process, which could be realized by microwave irradiation, pyrolysis or carbonization, hydrothermal or solvothermal reactions of organic precursors [5,33]. Generally favored by researchers, “bottom-up” synthesis strategies for CDs are easy, require inexpensive material and setup, being environmentally friendly and facilitate the introduction of multiple heteroatoms doping by easily inheriting organic precursors during the synthesis process [5,31,34]. These approaches can be achieved by chemical, electrochemical and physical methods, such as surface modification or functionalization, nanohybridisation and doping during the preparation of post-treatment of nanodots [35]. The yield of CDs could be improved by post-treatment or during arrangement. The CDs need to be modified to achieve benign surface properties that affect their solubility and functionality [5]. CDs can be synthesized using two types of precursors, either from chemical or green sources. The choice of precursor, synthesis conditions, and surface functionalization strategies significantly influence the physiochemical characteristics of the resulting CDs, encompassing aspects like size, crystallinity, oxygen or nitrogen content, size, quantum yield (QY), biocompatibility and colloidal stability [31]. In recent times, the emphasis has shifted towards simple, economical, and environmentally friendly synthesis methods to enable large-scale production of high-quality CDs.

Laser ablation is an example of “top-down” method for CDs synthesis. It represents a rapid and straightforward technique for synthesizing CDs, involving the irradiation of a carbon target with a laser [36]. While this method offers advantages in terms of simplicity and speed, it comes with significant drawbacks. For example, due to its high energy consumption, laser ablation is an energy-intensive process, which limits it practically for large-scale production [36–39]. Additionally, the process generally has a low QY, which affects the overall efficiency of CDs production. It is also difficult to control the size of the nanoparticles during laser ablation, resulting in variations in their size and morphology [36–39]. These limitations necessitate a thorough exploration of the method's parameters. For example, (1) optimization of laser parameters such as laser power, pulse duration, and wavelength (2) post-treatment method, surface passivation and functionalization with appropriate ligands (3) combination of laser ablation with other synthetic methods, such as chemical oxidation or microwave irradiation to better control over size, shape and QY of the CDs.

Chemical oxidation is a well-established “top-down” synthesis approach for CDs due to its cost efficiency, high production yield, high purity and easy size control [5,33,36]. It involves the oxidation of a carbon precursor using strong oxidant, resulting in CDs with abundant functional groups suitable for various applications, particularly in sensing technologies [5]. Various studies have demonstrated its efficacy by using strong oxidizing acids, such as HNO3 [40], H2SO4 [41], and H3PO2 [41,42]. CDs produced through chemical oxidation generally exhibit excellent photoluminescence properties, but the potential environmental hazards and impurity formation associated with strong acids are major drawbacks [5]. Additional processes are required to eliminate excess acid and impurities, increasing the overall cost of CDs production. [5] To overcome these environmental problems, an alternative approach involving using hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as an oxidizing agent has been proposed. Further research to develop more environmentally friendly chemical oxidation methods for CD synthesis is recommended to overcome these challenges in order to allow safer and more sustainable production practices while maintaining their desirable optical properties.

Ultrasonic treatment, as a “top-down” synthesis approach, employs high-energy ultrasonic waves to disintegrate large carbon molecules into smaller CDs particles, offering environmental friendliness, cost-effectiveness, and uniform results. Researchers have utilized this method for various carbon sources, including crab shells [43] and plant leaves [44], yielding highly fluorescent CDs with promising QY. The combination of ultrasonication with other techniques can enhance CDs performance, as seen in examples where CDs modified with copper tungstate exhibited efficient photocatalysis [45]. However, a significant challenge with ultrasonic treatment is the non-uniform heating of samples due to the local thermal effects of ultrasonic waves, impacting reaction efficiency when compared to direct heating or microwave methods [46,47]. To overcome this limitation, further research is necessary to improve the uniformity of heating in ultrasonic treatment, e.g. by optimizing the ultrasonic equipment and parameters, to achieve more uniform results, improve its potential as a green and efficient approach for CDs production.

Microwave irradiation synthesis is a fast, efficient, precisely temperature-controlled and environmentally friendly process for “bottom-up” synthesis of CDs from natural carbon precursors. Researchers have used this method to produce CDs from sources like banana peels [48], jackfruit seeds [49], quince fruit powder [50], and Gingko biloba leaves [51], yielding CDs particles of controlled size in high yield. However, microwave-synthesized CDs often exhibit lower QY compared to other methods, limiting their practical application. To address this limitation, a combination of microwave irradiation with other methods, like pyrolysis, is recommended to improve CDs QY. For instance, Gul et al. [52] have achieved high QY CDs from banana peels by first carbonizing the banana peels and then subjecting the powder to microwave irradiation, followed by filtration and centrifugation to remove larger particles. This two-step approach helps enhance the fluorescence efficiency of CDs produced through microwave irradiation. In addition, various parameter including reaction time, temperature, precursor concentration, and microwave power can be explored to optimize CDs synthesis for improved QY.

Pyrolysis or carbonization, a prevalent “bottom-up” approach for producing CDs, was originally proposed by Xu et al. [4] in the early stage of CDs discovery. In this methods, carbonaceous materials, especially natural products, are thermally decomposed at high temperature to produce carbon-rich materials that can subsequently be processed into CDs [53]. Pyrolysis is valued for its cost-effectiveness, eco-friendliness, and affordability, although it necessitates high temperatures for effective decomposition [53]. One of its primary advantages is its versatility, which allow the use of a wide range of carbon precursors, making it applicable to both natural sources and synthetic materials. This flexibility leads to a wide range of CDs with distinct properties and applications. Several studies have investigated the utilization of natural products such as flower [54], banana peels [52], gynostemma [55], and date palm fronds [56], for CDs synthesis through carbonization. For instance, Zhou et al. [57] developed high-quality fluorescent CDs using watermelon peels via a straightforward low-temperature carbonization filtration method, showcasing the potential of utilizing food waste as a sustainable carbon precursor. Similarly, Wei's group successfully generated CDs with strong blue fluorescence from gynostemma through pyrolysis, demonstrating the stability and low toxicity of the produced CDs [55]. Despite its simplicity and sustainability, pyrolysis-based CDs synthesis faces challenges related to controlling the size and size distribution of CDs, achieving high QY, and the substantial equipment requirements [46]. Achieving uniform-sized CDs can be problematic, as pyrolysis often results in a mixture of CDs sizes due to the complex thermal decomposition of the precursors. This lack of size homogeneity can be a significant drawback in applications that require CDs of specific dimensions, such as bioimaging or sensor development. To address this limitation, researchers have been exploring variations in the pyrolysis conditions, such as temperature, duration, and precursor materials, to optimize the CDs QY. Additionally, post-treatment and surface passivation techniques, such as modifying the CDs with appropriate functional groups or polymers, have been proposed to enhance their optical properties and QY. Continued research and development effort in this field aim to improve the efficiency and reliability of pyrolysis-based CDs synthesis, making it a more competitive and sustainable method for large-scale CD production.

Hydrothermal and Solvothermal Carbonization are popular “bottom-up” approaches that involve the preparation of CDs in high temperature and high-pressure aqueous or solvent environments. These methods are advantageous because they allow the production of CDs with tunable properties by adjusting parameters such as temperature, pressure, reaction time, and precursor composition. They also favored for their environmentally friendly, economical, non-toxic, and straightforward operational characteristics [35]. Solvothermal approach employs organic solvents as reaction medium, while the hydrothermal method generally use water, expanding the versatility of CDs for various applications. In solvothermal synthesis, for example, CDs have been successfully produced from banana juice, highlighting the simplicity and effectiveness of this method [58]. In hydrothermal synthesis, natural materials such as banana peels [59], waste tea [60], leaves [48,61–64], and more are utilized as carbon precursors to generate CDs. These CDs exhibit a variety of fluorescence properties, some of which can be used for applications such as Hg2 + detection [65]. However, a limitation of these methods lies in the variability in size, surface morphology, and controllability of CDs. In addition, the relatively high energy consumption due to the elevated temperature and pressure conditions can be a drawback in terms of sustainability. Researchers aim to address this limitation by exploring greener reaction conditions, utilizing microwave-assisted hydro/solvothermal synthesis, optimizing synthesis conditions, precursor choices, and passivation techniques to improve the CDs homogeneity and size control while minimizing the energy consumption and equipment requirements, ultimately enhancing their overall utility in various applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: As CDs have emerged as a promising nanomaterials class with unique properties, characterizing the properties of CDs is essential for understanding their structure, behaviour, optimizing their performance, and tailoring them for specific applications. Several characterization methods including fluorescence spectroscopy, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), zeta potential analysis, and QY analysis are employed to study the optical, structural and surface properties of CDs [5]. UV–vis spectroscopy is a fundamental technique to investigate the absorption and optical properties of CDs, given their robust UV absorption [5,27]. It involves shining UV light or visible light on a sample and measuring the absorbance or transmittance of light at different wavelengths, which will then result in the production of distinct spectra [5]. This technique is valuable for identifying the absorption peaks of CDs, typically the π-π* transition peak and the n-π* transition peak which corresponded to the transitions of C = C bonds and the C = O bonds that are commonly found in most CDs [5]. Additionally, fluorescence spectroscopy offers valuable insights into the photoluminescence mechanism of CDs. By exciting CDs with different wavelengths of light, their emission spectra change significantly, revealing characteristics of “excitation-wavelength-dependence” [66]. Fluorescence spectroscopy also provides insights into the quantum confinement effect and the nature of the emitting states in CDs, thus enabling researchers to understand the mechanisms behind their unique fluorescence behaviour [66]. Additionally, the structural characterization of CDs is essential to understand the particle size, shape, morphology, size distribution and crystalline organization of CDs [67,68]. Electron microscopic techniques such as TEM and SEM are commonly used to visualize the morphology and size distribution of CDs [67,68]. TEM offers higher precision when measuring CDs beyond their resolution limit [67,68]. This is because TEM can provide high-resolution images as it can afford higher resolving power, which allows researchers to observe individual CDs and analyze their size and shape accurately [67,68]. XRD is also employed to determine the crystallographic structure of CDs [23,27]. By exposing CDs to X-rays and analyzing the scattering patterns, researchers can determine the distribution of carbon atoms within the lattice and assess the degree of crystallinity in the materials [5,69]. In this case, XRD is particularly useful for understanding the graphitic nature of CDs and identifying specific crystallographic phases. FTIR is commonly used to investigate the functional groups on the surface of CDs [70]. It provides information about the chemical bonds and molecular vibrations, which are indicative of the presence of specific functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl or amino groups by analyzing the absorption of infrared light [5,27]. Thus, FTIR is essential for understanding the surface chemistry of CDs and their potential for functionalization. Moreover, zeta potential analysis is indispensable for studying the surface charge and colloidal stability of CDs in solution. By measuring the potential difference between the dispersion medium and the stationary fluid layer attached to the particle layer, zeta potential analysis yields insights into the stability and aggregation tendencies of CDs [5]. As outline by Sivasankaran et al. [71], a higher zeta potential value is indicative of system stability. In the context of nanoparticles, the positive or negative signs of zeta potential indicate the surface charges of these particles. When nanoparticles exhibit a low zeta potential value, they are more prone to aggregate. Thus, the analysis of zeta potential plays a critical role by providing crucial information concerning the stability and aggregation propensity of CDs, contributing to a comprehensive understanding of their behaviour in various contexts. Luminescence QY holds a pivotal role as a critical photophysical parameters in assessing the efficiency of fluorescence emission in CDs [5]. It quantifies the number of photons emitted by the CDs compared to the number of photons absorbed. High QY values are indicative of strong fluorescence emission, and such high-QY fluorophores find extensive applications across biomedical, optical and energy fields [5,72]. Therefore, QY analysis is essential for optimizing the photoluminescence properties of CDs and enhancing their performance in fluorescence-based applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges on carbon dots synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CDs fasces multifaceted challenges that require careful consideration for advancements in their applications. Low reaction yields are common issues in the synthesis of CDs. This challenge is related to the low efficiency of synthesis process to convert precursors materials into desired CDs. Many synthesis methods are inefficient, resulting in a limited quantity of CDs produced per reaction. Such low yield can hinder the scalability and cost-effectiveness of CDs production, necessitating the exploration of strategies to improve reaction efficiency. One strategy to address this challenge involves meticulous optimization of reaction parameters. Variables such as temperature, reaction time, and precursor concentrations play a key role in determining the efficiency of the synthesis process. Fine-tuning these variables can improve yields by ensuring that precursors are converted to CDs more efficiently. Exploring innovative precursor materials is another way to improve the efficiency of the reaction. Identifying precursors that are readily available and cost-effective, such as natural resources, can help to improve the sustainability and yield of synthetic methods. Furthermore, advances in automation and process control contribute to reproducibility and yield optimization. Overcoming the challenge of low reaction yields in CDs synthesis necessitates a comprehensive understanding of the underlying reaction mechanisms, coupled with systematic optimization of synthesis parameters to maximize the conversion of precursors into high-quality CDs, thus, ensuring a more efficient and scalable production process.

Another prominent challenge is the low reproducibility that cut across both “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches in traditional batch reactions [73]. Researchers often encounter batch-to-batch variations in size, surface functionalization, and optical properties. Furthermore, the variations in reaction conditions, precursor materials, and other factors can also lead to inconsistent results between batches. These inconsistencies hinder the reliable application of CDs and make it difficult to draw meaningful comparisons. To address this challenge, effort are being made to establish standardized and controlled synthesis protocols. The use of continuous reaction systems based on microfluidic technology is a promising approach that can significantly improving mixing efficiency and reducing reaction times [73]. This approach involves a one-step solvothermal processes in microreactors that ensures high-quality, reproducible, and homogeneous CDs production with optimized properties, making it suitable for scaled-up production of nanomaterials like nanometals [74], nanocomposites [75] and N-doped CDs [76]. In addition, precise control over precursor materials, reaction conditions, and passivation methods can help ensure consistent CDs production. Automation and optimization of the synthesis process are being explored to enhance reproducibility.

The low fluorescence QY of CDs is another concern. CDs are sought after for their potential use in bioimaging and other biomedical applications, but their performance can be limited by low fluorescence QY, especially in the biological transparency window (650–900 nm range) [77]. To mitigate this limitation, strategies such as atomic doping, acid assistance, and surface passivation have been explored to enhance fluorescence QY. Atomic doping involves the introduction of foreign atoms such as metallic and non-metallic elements into the carbon matrix [78]. Notable metallic dopants include Cu [79,80], Fe [81], Zn [78], superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs) [82,83] and rare earth elements like gold [84], lanthanum [85], gadolinium [86] and terbium [87]. These metallic dopants induce changes in the energy levels (from ground state (S0) to excited state), narrowing the band gap and enhancing the fluorescence properties of CDs [78]. Nitrogen doping is a prevalent non-metallic method that significantly affects the electron cloud density and electron energy levels of CDs, thereby increasing their QY [88–90]. Other non-metallic dopants, such as sulfur [91–93], phosphorus [94], boron [95], silicon [96], selenium [96,97], and halogen elements like fluorine [98–100], chlorine [100–102], bromine [100,103] and iodine [100], exhibit diverse modulation effects on the fluorescence properties of CDs. Understanding the unique impact of different dopants is crucial for tailoring CDs with specific optical properties to allow researchers to explore the potential for multimodal imaging using these doping agents. In addition to atomic doping, the assistance of acid in the formation of CDs has been shown to enhance their fluorescence performance. Among the acids, H2SO4 is particularly effective compared to HCl, HNO3, and HClO4 [104]. The non-volatility of H2SO4 and its ability to remain acidic throughout the reaction helps prevent the formation of large particles and facilitates the production of high-quality CDs, thus enhancing their fluorescence properties [78,105]. The surface properties of CDs play a pivotal role in their optical behavior, prompting extensive exploration of surface functionalization or passivation methods to enhance the QY of CDs. Given the abundant surface group on CDs, the functional ligands can readily bind through special interactions, such as coordination, amidation reactions, and electrostatic interaction [106]. Currently, various functional ligands including ions (Fe3+, Cu2+, Mn2+), organic molecules, polymers (polyethylene glycol (PEG), polyethylenimine (PEI)), DNA, and proteins [106,107], have been employed for surface modification of CDs. PEG, in particular, has been widely used as a passivation agent to enhance fluorescence intensity and ensure solution stability [78]. The passivation treatment with PEG not only boosts fluorescence but also demonstrated the ability to transformed non-fluorescent CDs into fluorescent or multicolor variants, highlighting the versatility of surface modification in modulating the optical properties of CDs [78]. The tailored surface functionalization allows for specific interactions with surrounding environments, influencing the electron cloud density and energy levels, and ultimately contributing to enhanced fluorescence performance. The combined strategies of atomic doping, acid-assisted, and surface passivation provides researchers with a comprehensive toolkit to design CDs with excellent fluorescence characteristics to meet the diverse requirements of various applications. These synergistic strategies offer a combination approach for multimodal imaging, where fluorescence, MRI, and photoacoustic signals can be harnessed simultaneously. These advancements hold great promise for applications in precision diagnostics, theranostic, and targeted imaging, showcasing the versatility of doped CDs in pushing the boundaries of multimodal imaging technologies.

Furthermore, diverse synthetic methods add to the complexity of the field. Many different approaches of CD synthesis exist, each with its own characteristics and properties. This diversity makes it very difficult to compare results and standardize CD production. The choice of synthetic method significantly influences the properties of the resulting CDs. To address this issue, researchers are working to consolidate and optimize synthetic methods. In-depth studies on the mechanisms and properties of CDs from various synthesis methods can help to provide comprehensive guidelines for selecting the appropriate method for a specific application. In addition, environmental concerns are of paramount importance. Several CDs synthesis methods utilizes potentially hazardous or environmentally unfriendly precursors and conditions. With increased environmental awareness, researchers have developed green synthesis methods to address these concerns. These greener approaches use benign precursors and more sustainable synthesis procedures to reduce the ecological footprint of CD production.

In summary, addressing the limitations of CD synthesis requires multidisciplinary efforts and collaborative research. The establishment of standardized protocols, optimization of synthetic conditions, enhancement of surface passivation, and development of tailored applications will contribute to the broader adoption of CDs and improved their performance, helping to overcome the challenges associated with reproducibility and fluorescence QY to propel the broader adoption of CDs across diverse applications. From bioimaging to drug delivery, the ability to tailor the properties of CDs positions them as promising nanomaterials for the future, contributing to the evolution of cutting-edge technologies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of carbon dots in anticancer therapy
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity is a critical aspect that researchers must consider when exploring the potential applications of CDs, especially in bioimaging and cell imaging. It refers to the ability of certain chemicals, mediators, or agents to induce cell death in living cells through various mechanisms, like apoptosis (programmed cell death) or necrosis (accidental cell death) [5,27]. The evaluation of CDs for their cytotoxicity in the context of anticancer therapy holds paramount significance in ensuring their safe and effective utilization. This intricate evaluation traverses both in vitro and in vivo domains, shedding light on the potential of CDs as selective cytotoxic agents against cancer cells while sparing normal cells, thereby offering promising avenues for advanced anticancer therapies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, and excretion of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Understanding the absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (ADME) of CDs is essential for evaluating their potential in a variety of applications, including drug delivery and imaging agents and other biomedical fields. While CDs exhibit significant promise, their pharmacokinetic properties play a crucial role in determining their effectiveness in clinical and therapeutic settings. Current research, both in vitro and in vivo, has shed light on the ADME profiles of CDs, offering their insights into their behaviour on biological systems.

The absorption of CDs plays a critical role in their pharmacokinetics. Reported in vitro and in vivo studies have demonstrated that CDs possess strong cellular uptake capabilities as they can be effectively absorbed by various cell types or biological systems, making them promising candidates for diverse applications. For example, Sun et al. [108] emphasized the efficient cellular uptake of CDs, particularly in ovarian serous cystadenocarcinoma cells (H0-8910). These findings emphasized the potential of CDs to be effectively absorbed by various cell types, making them suitable for intracellular applications, including drug delivery and bioimaging. The in vitro study conducted by Espina-Casado et al. [109] also revealed the absorption of CDs by different cell lines, such as murine melanoma cell line (B16F10), human osteosarcoma cell line (U2OS) and human mammary fibroblast (HMF). This cellular uptake is essential for applications such as intracellular pH sensing and bioimaging. These studies also underlined the strong potential of CDs to be absorbed by cellular systems, making them valuable tools for various applications. Furthermore, the ground-breaking research conducted by Zhang et al. [110] unveiled the intrinsic ability of specific CDs, such as aspirin-based CDs, to penetrate the blood–brain barrier (BBB) due to their small size and surface properties. The BBB is a significant physiological barrier that separates the blood in the capillary and interstitial fluid in the brain in order to restricts the entry of many substances into the central nervous system (CNS) and maintain the optimal microenvironment and function of CNS [110]. Overcoming this physiological barrier is a remarkable achievement, as it opens new avenues for targeted drug delivery to the brain, particularly for treatment of neuroinflammatory and neurodegenerative diseases. In addition, Yang et al. [111] reported that CDs derived from endogenous nutrients (glucose and tryptophan) exhibited visible and NIR fluorescence properties that facilitate their penetration of the BBB. Overall, CDs have shown exceptional capabilities in both cellular and physiological contexts, offering great promise in various biomedical applications.

The distribution of CDs within the biological system is crucial, and studies have demonstrated their efficient distribution, further underlying their potential in various applications. For instance, Thakur et al. [112] underscored the efficient distribution of GQDs in cells, including HeLa cervical cancer cell lines and MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells. This distribution capability contributes to the development of cancer theranostic applications, showcasing the potential of CDs in targeted therapies. Furthermore, Sun et al. [108] found that CDs can be employed as agents for intracellular drug delivery. The pH-assisted release of DOX from conjugated CDs reflected their role in facilitating intracellular drug distribution. In the in vitro cellular uptake and labelling study, Sun et al. [12] observed that CDs could be internalized into ovarian serous cystadenocarcinoma cells (H0-8910) and localized predominantly in the cytoplasm, confirming their intracellular distribution. This findings offer insights into the intracellular distribution of CDs, which is particularly valuable for various intracellular applications, including precision drug delivery. In addition to the CDs distribution within the cells, CDs have demonstrated effective distribution within the brain after crossing the BBB [110]. Zhang et al. [110] reported their successful distribution in the brain, which provides a promising avenue for targeted delivery of therapeutic agents for the treatment of neuroinflammatory and neurodegenerative diseases. The distribution of CDs within the brain was attributed to their surface nature and small size, which enable them to pass through the BBB [110]. Espina-Casado et al. [109] emphasized the in vivo distribution of CDs, demonstrating the effectiveness in living organisms. This aspect presents exciting possibilities for in vivo bioimaging and sensing, further emphasizing the versatility of CDs in various applications, ranging from cancer therapy to biomedical research.

The metabolic properties of CDs in biological systems are essential for understanding their pharmacokinetics. While CDs themselves are relatively stable, several studies have explored their potential therapeutic functionalities. Zhang et al. [110] discussed the intrinsic anti-inflammatory properties of aspirin-based CDs. These properties can influence cellular metabolism by reducing inflammation, suggesting an active role of CDs in cellular process. Additionally, CDs have been assessed for their capacity to carry and release therapeutic agents within cells. Sun et al. [108] demonstrated the effective internalization of CDs into cells for drug delivery, emphasizing their role in influencing cellular metabolic process. This interaction with cellular metabolism is particularly relevant for drug delivery applications, as CDs can influence drug metabolism and cellular responses. In the study conducted by Espina-Casado et al. [109], CDs were utilized for intracellular pH sensing. Their interaction with cellular processes, including pH sensing and bioimaging, demonstrates their metabolic involvement within cells. The metabolic interaction of CDs is crucial for drug delivery and intracellular application, offering for precision therapies.

Examining the excretion of CDs from biological systems is vital for assessing their pharmacokinetics and ensuring their safety and biocompatibility. Sun et al. [108] provide essential insights into the potential excretion of CDs through in vivo experiments. By monitoring the excretion of CDs after administration, they were able to understand the excretion pathways. Their findings showed that after injection, the fluorescence signals was stronger in the kidney and bladder, compared to other organs like the spleen, heart, and liver [108]. The fluorescence signal in the kidney increased significantly within 0.5 h after injection and gradually decreased after 1 h [108]. Subsequently, the fluorescence signal in the bladder gradually increased from 0.5 h to 1 h, indicating the delivery of CDs to the bladder from the kidney [108]. These results underscored the excretion and clearance of CDs from the body and highlighted their safety and biocompatibility. The ability of CDs to be eliminated from the body is a key factor in evaluating their suitability for biomedical applications, as it ensures minimal potential toxicity.

In summary, CDs exhibit impressive pharmacokinetic properties in terms of absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion. The reported studies reveal the immense potential of CDs in various biomedical applications, ranging from drug delivery to bioimaging, especially within neurological and cancer contexts. Understanding their cellular uptake, effective distribution, metabolic involvement, and excretion pathways is essential for maximizing their benefits and ensuring safety. The versatility and unique properties of CDs make them valuable tools in the biomedical field, offering exciting prospects for the development of precision therapies and diagnostic approaches. Further research is needed to delve deeper into specific mechanisms and challenges, paving the way for wider adoption in the field and the potential transformation of healthcare and medicine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity effect of carbon dots for anticancer therapeutics
ce:section.ce:para: In the realm of in vitro cytotoxicity assessments, CDs had undergone extensive scrutiny across a spectrum of cell lines, encompassing both cancer and normal cells. Employing a repertoire of cellular viability assay, such as cell courting kit-8 (CCK-8), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and water-soluble tetrazolium salt (WST-1) assay, CDs had consistently demonstrated commendable biocompatibility, eliciting low toxicity, and minimal adverse effects on healthy cells [113]. This has positioned them as promising candidates for bioimaging, drug delivery, and other therapeutic applications. Up to now, researchers had investigated the cytotoxicity of CDs synthesized using different carbon sources and preparation methods. For instance, CDs prepared from Gum Tragacanth (GT) and chitosan via a hydrothermal route showed excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity towards on human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC cells line) [114]. Similarly, CDs exhibiting blue fluorescence under the exposure of UV showcased outstanding biocompatibility with negligible cytotoxicity on human embryonic kidney normal cell lines (HEK 293) [115]. Moreover, in vitro cytotoxicity investigations of CDs on different cancer cell lines, such as human hepatic carcinoma (HepG2), human breast cancer cell (MCF-7) and noncancerous Chinese hamster ovarian (CHO) cell lines, had demonstrated that CDs showed very low cytotoxicity on normal cell lines (CHO) in comparison with cancer cell lines (MCF-7, HepG2) [116]. These results indicated that CDs have selective cytotoxic effects on cancer cells while showing minimal adverse effects on normal cells.

Further probing the selective cytotoxic potential of CDs on specific cancer cell lines had unveiled promising outcomes. CDs had been demonstrated efficacy in restraining the growth of diverse cancer types, including breast cancer, lung cancer, liver cancer, prostate cancer, and other malignancies (Table 1 ), indicates the potential of CDs to be harnessed for therapeutic purposes. The cytotoxicity attributed to CDs was primarily rooted in the production of ROS within the cells, inducing oxidative stress and apoptosis [7–9]. For example, Li et al. [8] successfully proved the fluorescent CDs prepared from fresh tender ginger juice exhibited significant suppression of HepG2 growth with low toxicity in comparison with normal mammalian cells such as normal liver cells (FL83B) and mammary epithelial cells (MCF-10A). In this study, the 50 % inhibitory concentration (IC50) value of CDs on HepG2 cells was 350 μg/mL[8]. Li et al. [8] found that the cytotoxic effects of CDs on liver cancer cells was linked to ROS generation, inducing oxidative stress and apoptosis by triggering the pro-apoptotic factor and inhibiting nutrient influx through membrane attachment [8]. In addition, Xie et al.[7] also managed to synthesize CDs through a hydrothermal route using date pits pre-treated with phosphoric acid as a carbon source. The CDs showed potent inhibition activity towards MCF-7 human lung cancer (A549) and human prostate cancer (PC3) cells [7]. However, the inhibition was selective to cancer cells only as only a very low inhibition effect was observed for kidney (HEK293) cells at the same dose. They also found that the cytotoxicity of CDs was mainly attributed to the generation of ROS in the cells induced by the CDs [7]. The attachment of CDs to the cancer cell’s membrane would likely prevent the influx of nutrients from entering the cancer cells which eventually kills the cancer cells [7]. Similarly, Arkan et al. [9] utilized walnut oil as the carbon source to obtain fluorescence CDs via hydrothermal synthesis. The MTT assay was then performed to investigate the cytotoxic and apoptogenic activities of the newly obtained CDs towards three human cancer cell lines: PC3, MCF-7 and human colorectal adenocarcinoma cell (HT-29)[9]. The as-prepared walnut CDs evidenced to be an outstanding potent cytotoxic agent, especially against PC3 and MCF-7 cell lines as confirmed by its IC50 value (<10 μg/cc) [9]. Interestingly, they found that the exposure of CDs on PC3 and MCF-7 cells caused an increase in the caspase-3 level which will then lead to the induction of apoptosis [9].

Moreover, certain studies also highlighted the unique pharmacological effects of CDs, especially when doped with certain elements, exhibiting unique features that promote ROS generation within cancer cells. The incorporation of various dopants, such as phosphorus, copper, and nitrogen, imparts distinctive properties to CDs, making them versatile candidates for cancer therapy. For instance, Bajpai et al. [117] delves the multifunctional properties of nitrogen-phosphorus-doped (N-P-doped) CDs and their impact on B16F10 melanoma cancer cells. The research explores the regulation of apoptosis and autophagy, key processes in determining cell fate. The nitrogen and phosphorus doping likely contribute to the enhanced anticancer effects. The mechanism underlying the cytotoxicity might involve intricate interactions leading to increased levels of ROS, further inducing apoptosis in cancer cells [117]. Treatment with N-P-doped also resulted in dose-dependent cell cycle arrest and autophagy [117]. Additionally, the study emphasizes the potential of these doped CDs for in vitro imaging applications, showcasing their diagnostic utility [117]. Another investigation involving nitrogen and copper-doped saffron-based CDs sheds light on their cytotoxic effects on human colorectal cancer cells, specifically HCT-116 and HT-29 [118]. The incorporation of nitrogen and copper may impart unique properties to the CDs, potentially enhancing their ability to induce cytotoxicity. The observed cytotoxic effects in this study may involve mechanisms such as increased ROS generation which play a crucial role in inducing oxidative stress within the cells [118]. This oxidative stress can lead to various cellular responses, including damage to cellular components as well as driving cellular signaling and, in the context of cancer cells, potentially inducing cell death (apoptosis). The study also collectively underscored the versatility of doped CDs in cancer therapy, showcasing their multifunctionality and their potential to modulate essential cellular processes in cancer cells. These findings contribute to the growing understanding of doped CDs as promising tools in the intricate landscape of cancer therapy, offering a nuanced approach to targeting cancer cells with enhanced precision.

Accordingly, these intricate findings illuminate CDs' potential as potent cytotoxic agents against cancer cells while preserving normal cell viability, propelling them into the realm of promising candidates for future anticancer therapeutics. The selective induction of apoptosis in cancer cells, along with the multifunctionality of doped CDs, suggests their potential for targeted and mechanism-driven cancer therapies. While the realm of in vitro investigations provides a robust foundation, the imperative to bridge this knowledge to in vivo scenarios becomes paramount for validating the safety and efficacy of CDs within the intricate milieu of cancer therapy. The path forward necessitates persistent research efforts aimed at unravelling the underlying mechanisms of CDs' cytotoxicity, alongside comprehensive and meticulously designed in vivo studies. These endeavors are pivotal to substantiating CDs' potential and propelling their seamless integration into the realm of nanomedicine for cancer therapy. Undoubtedly, the demonstrated biocompatibility and minimal toxicity of CDs on normal cells, coupled with their selective cytotoxicity against cancer cells, resonate as a clarion call for the exploration of myriad biomedical applications. Among these, the potential for CDs to revolutionize established paradigms within cancer therapy looms prominently. The fusion of CDs' multifaceted attributes with innovative therapeutic strategies holds the promise of ushering in transformative changes in the landscape of cancer treatment. This convergence not only underscores CDs' potential as a dynamic therapeutic tool but also highlights the imperative for a holistic understanding of their behaviour in vivo. In summation, the intricate dance of CDs' cytotoxicity, orchestrating the demise of cancer cells while preserving normal cellular viability, positions them as formidable contenders in the realm of future anticancer therapeutics. Their precision in triggering apoptosis within cancer cells and the strategic path to in vivo validation underscore the significance of ongoing research endeavors. As this journey unfolds, the potential for CDs to reshape the contours of cancer therapy paradigms stands illuminated, propelled by their proven biocompatibility, minimal toxicity on normal cells, and targeted cytotoxicity on cancer cells. In essence, the journey of CDs from in vitro insights to in vivo validation converges towards a promising horizon where biomedical applications, particularly in cancer therapy, are poised for transformation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro combination therapies with carbon dots: Synergizing chemotherapy, radiotherapy and targeted therapy
ce:section.ce:para: In the dynamic landscape of cancer therapy, the concept of combination therapy had emerged as a promising avenue, aiming to enhance therapeutic efficacy, minimize drug resistance, and mitigate adverse side effects. Indeed, the convergence of CDs with diverse cancer treatment modalities such as chemotherapy, radiotherapy and targeted drug delivery, holds great promise in enhancing the therapeutic outcome of cancer treatment [1]. The inherent versatility and efficacy of CDs in preclinical investigation lend credence to their potential as potent adjuncts in the field of cancer therapeutics.

Chemotherapy remains a primary treatment option for various cancers. However, its efficacy is often limited by drug resistance and systemic toxicity. In this case, CDs have shown promise in enhancing the therapeutic outcomes of chemotherapy through various mechanisms as they can be used as nanocarriers for chemotherapy, resulting in enhancing therapeutic outcomes for cancer therapy. As an example, the research conducted by Yang et al. [13] presented a noteworthy and promising approach in the field of cancer therapy by combining CDs with the chemotherapy drug doxorubicin (DOX) to achieve a synergistic effect. In this study, DOX was loaded onto the CDs surface derived from folic acid (FA) through electrostatic interaction to create a nano-drug formulation called CDs/DOX [13]. The findings of the study demonstrated that the prepared nano-drug CDs/DOX could effectively release DOX under mild pH stimulation [13]. The in vitro studies on HeLa cervical cancer cells confirmed that CDs/DOX can selectively accumulate in the tumor cells with high expression of folate receptors (FR) and release the drug (DOX) into the nucleus [13]. The in vitro studies also showed that CDs/DOX formulation exhibited a significant increase in cancer cell killing compared to free DOX [13]. This highlights the potential of CDs as targeted drug carriers for chemotherapy, enhancing the toxicity of DOX to tumor cells and potentially reducing systemic toxicity. Similarly, Mahani et al. [120] reported on the combination of CDs modified by transferrin (TF) with the chemotherapy drug DOX. The findings of the study demonstrated that CDs efficiently encapsulated DOX and exhibited pH-responsive drug release behaviour [120]. This pH-responsive drug release is particularly advantageous as it allowed selective drug release in the acidic tumor microenvironment, leading to improved drug delivery and therapeutic efficiency. As a result, the DOX-loaded TF-CDs can selectively release the drug in the tumor site, leading to enhanced drug delivery and improved therapeutic efficacy [120]. The in vitro studies on the DOX-loaded TF-CQDs formulation showed enhanced cellular uptake and cytotoxicity in the MCF-7 compared to free DOX [120]. This was a crucial observation as CDs were able to improve cellular uptake of the drug, contributing to more effective cancer cell killing. Accordingly, these studies collectively demonstrated the synergistic effect of combining CDs with chemotherapy drugs like DOX. By using CDs as nanocarriers, drug delivery can be improved, increasing drug accumulation at the tumor site, and enhancing the intracellular drug release. This approach also had the potential to improve the overall efficacy of chemotherapy while minimizing systemic toxicity.

Radiation therapy or radiotherapy is a mainstay in cancer treatment that employs ionizing radiation to damage and kill cancer cells. The propensity to damage healthy tissues alongside cancer cells prompts the need for innovative strategies to enhance its efficacy. In this context, CDs had manifested as effective radiosensitizers. For instance, Kleinauskas et al. [121] explored the potential application of carbon-core silver-shell nanodots (CDs) as radiosensitizers for both phototherapy and radiotherapy. The in vitro investigation on prostate adenocarcinoma cell lines (Du145) demonstrated the enhanced cytotoxicity of CDs under light irradiation, positioning them as potent light sensitizers for phototherapy [121]. Intriguingly, the same CDs demonstrated a capacity to sensitize cancer cells to low-energy X-ray radiation, offering a dual approach to cancer cell demise when combined with radiotherapy [121]. The study’s in vitro finding provides valuable insight into the potential clinical applications of these CDs in cancer treatment, paying the way for further research and development in the field of cancer therapeutics. Subsequently, Ma et al. [14] also explored the application of gadolinium-intercalated CDs (Gd@C-dots) as sensitizers to enhance radiotherapy for lung cancer cells (H1299). They found that the unique properties of Gd@C-dots, which were composed of gadolinium intercalated into CDs, offer several benefits over conventional high Z-nanoparticles (HZNPs), including significant stability, minimal leakage and remarkable efficiency as radiosensitizers [14]. The choice of Gd@C-dots as sensitizers demonstrated their potential as an effective and safe therapeutic option. Additionally, the in vitro studies with H1299 cell lines treated with pPD-Gd@C-dots in combination with the radiation resulted in improved tumor suppression compared to the radiation alone [14]. This suggested that the combined therapy of pPD-Gd@C-dots and radiotherapy leads to enhanced cancer cell death, offering a promising approach for effective combination therapy. Overall, the studies by Kleinauskas et al. [121] and Ma et al. [14] demonstrated the ability of CDs to enhance the cytotoxicity of cancer cells under both phototherapy and radiotherapy conditions. This dual approach offers a promising strategy for effectively killing cancer cells while minimizing damage to surrounding healthy tissues. The unique properties of Gd@C-dots explored by Ma et al. [14] further highlighted the potential of CDs as safe and efficient radiosensitizers.

Targeted drug delivery using CDs has also shown great potential for improving therapeutic outcomes while minimizing off-target effects. Functionalization of CDs with specific ligands or antibodies allows for selective targeting of cancer cells, as demonstrated by Paliwal et al. [122]. The researchers reported the development of FA conjugated CDs to achieve targeted drug delivery of capecitabine (CAP) to colorectal cancer cells [122]. In this case, CDs were prepared by microwave irradiation method followed by the sonication method to achieve FA-CDs [122]. The CAP was then loaded onto the surface of FA-CDs through electrostatic interactions. The CAP capped passivated CDs (CAP-FA-CDPs) formulation showed enhanced cellular uptake and cytotoxicity in FA-positive cancer cells (HT-29) and was further confirmed by the ROS assay and flow cytometry analysis as a noteworthy increase in the apoptotic cells was observed in CAP-FA-CDPs treated cells [122]. The combination of targeted drug delivery with CDs and chemotherapy led to enhanced cellular uptake, increased cytotoxicity in cancer cells, minimized off-target effects and reduced cytotoxicity in healthy cells, indicating the safety and effectiveness of this approach.

Overall, the preclinical studies presented underscore the potential of CDs as effective nanomaterials in combination therapy. The ability of CDs to serve as drug carriers and radiosensitizers makes them valuable candidates for the development of targeted and personalized cancer treatment strategies. As research in this field progresses, it is expected that CDs will play an increasingly important role in advancing cancer therapeutics, bringing us closer to more effective and personalized approaches in the fight against cancer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo cytotoxicity studies of carbon dots for anticancer therapeutics
ce:section.ce:para: CDs have garnered attention as a promising nanomaterial with low toxicity, making them suitable for various biomedical applications. While a considerable amount of research has been conducted on the in vitro cytotoxicity assessment of CDs, there remains a limited number of studies focusing on in vivo evaluations. In vivo toxicity investigations involving CDs are generally conducted after their injection into the body. The present in vivo studies on animal models have demonstrated the biocompatibility of CDs, with minimal adverse effects observed even at higher doses. In 2018, Wang et al. [123] conducted a study to investigate the biosafety of Hypocrella bambusae-derived CDs (HBCDs) in mice. They performed in vivo imaging of tumors and major organs at different intervals after intravenous injection of HBCDs [123]. Compared to the untreated control group, the researchers found no significant histological changes in the treated mice [123]. This result demonstrated the low side effects and excellent biocompatibility of HBCDs in vivo. Additionally, monitoring the mice’s body weight during HBCDs treatment revealed no abrupt alterations, indicating the low biotoxicity of HBCDs for in vivo treatment [123]. More recently, in 2019, Singh et al. [6] studied the distribution of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) in mice after injection. The researchers evaluated the in vivo toxic effects of NCQDs over 30 days in mice at two concentrations (5.0 mg/kg body weight and 10.0 mg/kg body weight) of NCQDs [6]. They analyzed various parameters such as histopathological, antioxidant, serum biochemical, and hematological parameters [6]. These studies affirmed the notion that NCQDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility. This finding further supported the notion that CDs had low cytotoxicity and were well-tolerated in vivo. Accordingly, these studies highlighted the promising biocompatibility and low toxicity of CDs in in vivo settings. The lack of evident histological alterations and stable body weights in mice treated with CDs suggested their potential as safe nanomaterials for biomedical applications. However, it is important to acknowledge that more comprehensive in vivo toxicity studies, especially with different injection doses and time durations, are necessary to fully understand the safety profile of CDs. Conducting further research on the biodistribution, long-term effects, and clearance of CDs will provide valuable insights for their future clinical translation.

In addition to their low toxicity, CDs had shown antitumor potency in certain in vivo studies. For example, Lu et al. [124] successfully synthesized gallic acid-based CDs (GACDs) through a microwave irradiation approach and investigated their in vivo antitumor efficacy. The GACDs were found to significantly reduced tumor growth in a tumor-bearing mouse model. This antitumor effect was more pronounced compared to pure gallic acid and the negative control group treated with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) [124]. Lu et al. [124] reported that the enhanced antitumor effect of GACDs could be attributed to the enlargement of gallic acid upon incorporation into CDs. This enlargement might lead to improve drug circulation and higher drug accumulation at the tumor sites, resulting in better therapeutic efficacy [124]. Importantly, GACDs showed excellent biocompatibility, causing no detectable damage to normal tissues and organs [124]. This finding indicated the safe use of GACDs in vivo, minimizing undesirable side effects. Overall, the in vivo antitumor experiments demonstrated that GACDs hold significant therapeutic efficacy with minimal side effects, making them promising candidates for further development in cancer therapy. In another study conducted by Zhao et al. [125], P-CDs were synthesized from procaine and evaluated for their in vivo antitumor effect. Nude mice bearing DLD1 cells were treated with P-CDs, procaine (PCA), or PBS as a negative control [125]. The results showed that the PCDs significantly reduced DLD1 tumor development after in vivo treatment compared to the PBS and PCA groups [125]. This observation highlighted the potential of P-CDs as effective agents for inhibiting tumor growth in vivo. Additionally, histochemical analyses of major organs, such as the liver, heart, lungs, kidneys and spleen, revealed no pathological changes in the P-CDs and PCA groups in comparison with the control group [125]. Collectively, in vivo antitumor experiments illustrated that P-CDs hold significant therapeutic efficacy with minimal side effects, positioning them as promising candidates for further advancement in cancer therapy.

Accordingly, CDs had shown great promise as a nanomaterial with low cytotoxicity and significant antitumor potency in in vivo studies. Their excellent biocompatibility and minimal side effects make them suitable candidates for safe and effective in vivo treatment. Studies investigating the in vivo toxicity of CDs, such as HBCDs and NCQDs, have yielded valuable insights into their biocompatibility and safety in animal models. These findings support the potential of CDs for various biomedical applications, including cancer therapy. The examples of GACDs and P-CDs demonstrated the potential of CDs as effective and safe nanomaterials for cancer treatment. GACDs exhibited significant antitumor efficacy and retained the excellent biocompatibility of gallic acid. P-CDs, on the other hand, remarkably inhibited tumor growth without causing organ toxicity. Despite the promising results, further research is necessary to comprehensively evaluate the in vivo toxicity of CDs across different injection doses and exposure durations. Understanding the long-term effects and biodistribution of CDs in vivo is essential for their safe clinical translation. As research in the realm of nanomedicine continues to advance, CDs hold substantial potential for revolutionizing cancer therapy. Their capacity to be functionalized and targeted to specific tumor sites, combined with their low toxicity, opens up new possibilities for personalized and targeted cancer treatment. Overall, CDs represent a promising avenue for future biomedical research and clinical therapeutics, offering the potential for safer and more effective anticancer treatments with reduced side effects and improved patient outcomes. With ongoing research and development, CDs have the potential to become valuable tools in the fight against cancer and other diseases, bringing us closer to a brighter and healthier future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo combination therapies with carbon dots: Synergizing chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and immunotherapy
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the landscape of cancer treatment has experienced a paradigm shift with the emergence of combination therapies, aiming to achieve more potent and comprehensive strategies to tackle the complexity of cancer progression. The arena of in vivo combination therapy using CDs for cancer treatment has witnessed significant advancements, particularly in the domains of chemotherapy, radiotherapy, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy. The distinctive attributes of CDs, encompassing biocompatibility, low toxicity, tunable surface chemistry, and fluorescence capabilities, have positioned them as promising candidates for multifaceted therapeutic approaches in cancer management [5].

CDs have the potential to serve as vehicles for therapeutic payloads, such as chemotherapeutic agents, and deliver them selectively to tumor cells. This precision minimizes off-target effects and enhances drug accumulation at the tumor site. A notable exemplar is the work of Wang et al. [10], who engineered a drug delivery system for liver cancer employing highly luminescent CDs conjugated with FA. The folate-conjugated CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence properties for enhanced imaging and specifically targeted liver cancer cells (HepG2) due to their FA affinity. The CDs loaded with the chemotherapeutic drug DOX demonstrated efficient targeted delivery, resulting in enhanced therapeutic effects and reduced systemic toxicity in a liver cancer mouse model [10]. Thus, the study underscores the potential of CDs to elevate both imaging precision and therapeutic efficacy in liver cancer treatment. Meanwhile, Tejwan et al. [126] presented a pH-responsive and targeted drug delivery system for breast cancer therapy using FA-functionalized CDs. The CDs were loaded with the natural flavonoid, rutin (RUT), renowned for its anticancer properties. The FA functionalization facilitated specific targeting of MCF-7 that overexpressed FR [126]. The system exhibited pH-dependent drug release behaviour, ensuring drug release in the acidic tumor microenvironment [126]. The combination of targeted delivery and pH-responsive release resulted in enhanced tumor suppression and reduced off-target effects in a breast cancer mouse model compared to a single treatment [126]. Crucially, both nano-formulation (FA-CDs-RUT) and bare RUT showed excellent biocompatibility, causing no detectable alteration in blood biochemical parameters [126]. This attests to the safety of FA-CDs-RUT in vivo, minimizing undesirable side effects. Moreover, this study also indicated the potential of CDs to enable pH-responsive and targeted drug delivery for breast cancer therapy. The incorporation of natural compounds and the synergistic effect of targeted delivery and pH-responsive release using CDS offer a promising avenue for improving breast cancer treatment.

In addition, the amalgamation of CDs with radiotherapy also displayed promise in enhancing treatment outcomes. For instance, Du et al. [11] addressed the potential of gadolinium-doped CDs (Gd-CDs) as versatile nanomaterials for combining radiotherapy with magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) guidance. The evaluation of Gd-CDs in an in vivo setting involved their administration to tumor-bearing mice, followed by MRI imaging and radiotherapy [11]. The results revealed that Gd-CDs offered enhanced tumor contrast in MRI, enabling accurate tumor delineation. Moreover, the combination of Gd-CDs and radiotherapy led to improved therapeutic outcomes, including enhanced tumor growth inhibition and increased survival rates [11]. Similarly, Ma et al. [14] delved into the strategic use of gadolinium-intercalated CDs (Gd@C-dots) to enhance the effectiveness of radiotherapy specifically for H1299 non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). In vivo experiments were conducted using NSCLC-bearing mice, where Gd@C-dots were administered prior to radiotherapy [14]. The findings indicated that Gd@C-dots enhanced the tumor's response to radiotherapy, leading to increased tumor growth inhibition and prolonged survival of the treated mice [14]. Importantly, the study emphasized the potential of Gd@C-dots in personalized treatment, leveraging image-guided methodologies for tailoring therapies to individual patients.

Tumor immunotherapy had gained significant attention for its efficient targeting and lasting curative effect. In a departure from conventional cancer therapies, such as surgery, radiotherapy and chemotherapy, tumor immunotherapy aimed to boost the patient’s immune system to recognize and attack cancer cells [12]. Owing to highly enriched distinctive physical and chemical characteristics, CDs had also shown potential in synergistic treatment involving PDT and immunotherapy. Kim et al. [127] engineered a novel system using pH-sensitive CDs loaded with Chlorin e6 (Ce6) to establish superior antitumor immunity. Ce6 was a well-known PS used in PDT for targeting epidermal growth factor (EGF) located within the cancer cells [128]. The CDs exhibited a selective release of Ce6 in the acidic pH of the tumor microenvironment (pH 6.5) compared to the physiological pH (pH 7.4) [127]. In a mouse model with bilateral CT-26 tumors, the combination of Chlorin e6-loaded, imidazole-doped, pH-sensitive CDs (Ce6@IDCDs) and laser treatment showed significant antitumor effects at laser-treated primary tumor regions via ROS generation [127]. Moreover, the combination therapy further stimulated immune cells’ recruitment (including activated CD8 + T cells, natural killer (NK) cells, and mature dendritic cells (DCs)) into the tumor tissue, leading to enhanced antitumor immune response and growth inhibition [127]. In another study by Zhang et al. [129], a multifunctional nanoparticle system composed of γ-polyglutamic acid (γ-PGA), glucose oxidase (GOx), and manganese and copper-doped CDs (γ-PGA@GOx@Mn,Cu-CDsMn,Cu-CDs) was developed. This nanoparticle system showed a prolonged retention time at the tumor microenvironment (TME) and targeted cancer cells. Under laser irradiation at 730 nm, the nanoparticle system exhibited both photothermal and photodynamic effects [129]. The combination of this nanoparticle system with checkpoint blockade therapy activated a systematic antitumor immune response, leading to the elimination of primary and distant tumors. The CDs in this context acted as PS and nanocarriers, facilitating the synergy between PDT and immunotherapy [129].

Overall, the integration of CDs into in vivo combination therapies has opened new avenues for cancer treatment. From enhancing chemotherapy and radiotherapy outcomes to synergizing with immunotherapy, CDs have demonstrated their versatility and potential in revolutionizing cancer therapy approaches. As research in this area continues, the potential clinical translation of CDs-based combination therapy holds promise for improving cancer treatment outcomes and patient outcomes.
