Nevertheless, further preclinical and clinical studies are necessary to fully evaluate the safety, efficacy, and long-term effects of these innovative approaches before their application in routine clinical practice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of carbon dots in photo-induced cancer treatment
ce:section.ce:para: The landscape of cancer treatment has undergone significant advancements, marked by a shift towards targeted and minimally invasive approaches. While traditional treatments such as surgery, chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and immunotherapy have played crucial roles, they often come with debilitating side effects, limited selectivity for targeting cancer cells and hindering effective patient recovery [15]. To address these limitations, novel strategies are being explored, and phototherapy, encompassing both PDT and PTT, has emerged as a promising alternative. Its non-invasive nature, low toxicity, and potential for combination with other therapeutic modalities make it an attractive avenue in the fight against cancer [15].(See Fig. 1 ).

While various organic PS, such as porphyrin [133], hypocrellin [134,135], and phthalocyanine [136], had been employed in PDT for their efficient 1O2 generation, their limitations including poor water solubility and lack of tumor-targeting ability necessitate the search for alternative materials [15,137]. Although gold nanostructures and semiconductor nanomaterials exhibit strong absorbance and photothermal efficiency, their clinical application is hindered by high toxicity [15,138,139]. In this pursuit, CDs, a class of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, have emerged as promising candidates for photo-induced cancer treatment, encompassing both PDT and PTT. CDs possess remarkable properties such as outstanding water solubility, biocompatibility, tunable photoluminescence, strong absorption (visible -NIR region), as well as heat and ROS generation, positioning them as potential game-changers for augmenting the precision and effectiveness of cancer treatment [5]. Recent discoveries had revealed that CDs can function as effective PS, generating ROS upon light excitation. Additionally, they can serve as PA, converting light energy into heat [15]. This dual capability equips CDs for applications in both PDT, inducing cancer cell death via ROS, and PTT, locally ablating cancer cells through heat generation [15]. In this section, we will delve into the diverse applications of CDs in photo-induced cancer treatment, exploring their mode of action and providing substantiation through relevant research studies. Through these explorations, our goal is to shed light on the promising role that CDs can play in revolutionizing cancer treatment strategies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mode of action of CDs in phototherapy
ce:section.ce:para: CDs, with their distinctive optical properties, emerges as promising candidates for applications in PTT and PDT, key modalities in cancer treatment. These nanoscale carbon-based materials exhibit strong absorption in the UV to visible range, attributed to the π-π* transition in the carbon core. Thus, the mode of action of CDs in phototherapy is multifaceted and stems from their unique optical properties and interactions with light. CDs can serve as both PS for PDT and PA for PTT, making them versatile tools for cancer treatment [5]. Their interactions with light lead to the generation of ROS in PDT and localized heating in PTT, ultimately resulting in cancer cell destruction.

In PTT, CDs excel as efficient PA that can absorb light and convert it into heat [15]. The unique optical properties of CDs that mainly attributed to the π-π* transition in the carbon core enables CDs to absorb light in the UV and NIR regions of the electromagnetic spectrum, which are known for their tissue-penetrating properties [140]. Upon light absorption, the energy absorbed from the protons is rapidly converted into heat due to nonradiative transitions, leading to an increase in temperature within the CDs and their surrounding environment [148]. This absorption capability facilitates efficient photothermal conversion, allowing CDs to selectively generate heat. The localized heat generated by CDs in response to light absorption leads to a rapid increase in temperature within the tumor region and kills the tumor cells [148]. This rise in temperature is critical for inducing hyperthermia, a therapeutic effect where temperatures are elevated to levels that can induce irreversible cellular damage or even death of cancer cells [149]. Cancer cells are particularly susceptible to elevated temperatures due to their relatively higher metabolic rates, poor heat dissipation mechanisms, and hypoxic environment compared to healthy cells [150]. The localized hyperthermia induced by CDs in PTT triggers a cascade of cellular responses. Cancer cells experience stress as a result of the temperature increase, leading to denaturation of proteins, disruption of cell membranes, and mitochondria dysfunction [151]. Additionally, localized hyperthermia can lead to changes in the tumor microenvironment, enhancing blood flow and oxygen supply to the tumor, decreasing the interstitial fluid pressure, and restoring the normal physiological pH [152]. The combination of cellular stress, disruption of critical processes, and changes in the tumor microenvironment can ultimately drive cancer cells towards cell death (Fig. 4 ). Cell death via PTT occurs at temperatures above 42 °C and involves two main pathways which are necrosis and apoptosis [153]. Recently, a tightly controlled cell death process called necroptosis has been identified in PTT, resembling unregulated necrosis [153]. At 41 °C, rapid changes in gene expression, including heat-shock proteins, are triggered to counter initial thermal damage [153]. Irreversible tissue damage occurs at 42 °C, and heating to around 46 °C induces necroptosis and apoptosis, while temperatures above 49 °C lead to cell necrosis [153]. Apoptosis is a well-controlled and programmed form of cell death, characterized by cytoplasmic shrinkage, chromatin condensation, membrane blebbing, DNA fragmentation and apoptotic bodies formation [154]. Necrosis, on the other hand, is a more chaotic and uncontrolled form of cell death, often associated with the disruption of the cell membrane, release of cellular contents and inflammation [153]. The heat-induced cellular stress and death caused by CDs in PTT can also activate the immune system. The release of cellular debris and signalling molecules from dying cancer cells can attract immune cells to the tumor site [155]. This immune response may contribute to the destruction of remaining cancer cells and potentially lead to a more robust and durable antitumor immune response [155]. In addition, PTT using CDs can be combined with other therapeutic approaches, such as chemotherapy or immunotherapy, to enhance overall treatment efficacy [155]. The localized heat generated by CDs can sensitize cancer cells to other treatments, making PTT a valuable addition to multidisciplinary cancer treatment strategies.

Accordingly, the mode of action of CDs in phototherapy underscores their dual capability as both PS and PA, enabling the generation of ROS in PDT and localized heating in PTT. These interactions with light lead to a comprehensive approach to cancer cell destruction. CDs can be designed to possess both photothermal and photodynamic effects simultaneously, creating a synergistic approach that enhances therapeutic efficacy. Additionally, the ability of CDs to combine with other therapeutic modalities, such as chemotherapy or immunotherapy, further highlights their potential as a valuable addition to multidisciplinary cancer treatment strategies. With their selective cytotoxicity and tunable properties, CDs represent a promising frontier in the development of advanced cancer treatment modalities, offering precise and effective therapeutic interventions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo studies of carbon dots in photodynamic therapy
ce:section.ce:para: The utilization of CDs in PDT has garnered substantial attention due to their exceptional photophysical attributes, encompassing robust absorption across the UV and NIR regions, as well as efficient fluorescence emission. These characteristics position CDs as promising candidates for PDT, wherein they operate PS capable of absorbing light energy. This process triggers electron excitation and subsequent energy transfer to neighbouring oxygen molecules, thereby inducing the generation of ROS that induce oxidative stress and activate cell death pathways. The efficacy of CDs in PDT is underscored by a compilation of in vitro and in vivo investigations, as illustrated in Table 2 and Table 3 , focusing on their role as PS. Successful PDT outcomes also rely on the judicious selection of light source and energy, often involving lasers or light-emitting diodes (LEDs) emitting specific wavelengths, matched to the absorption characteristics of the loaded PS-loaded CDs. Notably, the study conducted by Yue et al. [156] delved into the intricate interplay between CDs and PDT through a comprehensive examination of their in vitro and in vivo applications. The in vitro component of the study focused on the synthesis and characterization of riboflavin-based CDs (RCDs) as potential PS for PDT. Through a meticulous evaluation of their 1O2 generation capacity, the study established the inherent ability of RCDs to produce reactive ROS upon laser excitation (365 nm laser, from 1 min to 20 min) [156]. Cellular experiments involving 4 T1 mouse breast cancer cell lines provided compelling evidence of the RCDs' capability to induce cytotoxicity upon PDT treatment [156]. The results shaded light on the intricate molecular processes triggered by RCDs during PDT, emphasizing their potential as effective therapeutic agents for targeting cancer cells [156]. The transition to the in vivo domain extended the investigation to animal models, aiming to validate the PDT efficacy of RCDs within a physiological context. Tumor-bearing mice were intravenously administered with RCDs, followed by laser irradiation to activate the photosensitizing effect [156]. The in vivo studies showcased the accumulation of RCDs within tumor tissues and subsequent ROS generation upon laser exposure, leading to tumor growth inhibition [156]. The controlled and targeted nature of this therapeutic approach highlighted the promise of RCDs in mediating effective PDT outcomes in vivo [156]. The synergistic combination of 1O2 generation capacity observed in vitro and the therapeutic outcomes demonstrated in vivo underscore the translational potential of RCDs for clinical PDT applications. Furthermore, the study's used of riboflavin as a precursor for RCDs adds an element of biocompatibility and safety to the PDT approach, enhancing its attractiveness for clinical translation.

Furthermore, Yang et al. [157] explored the utility of CDs derived from tea polyphenol as potential PS in PDT. Through a comprehensive investigation that spanned both in vitro and in vivo experiments, the researchers meticulously assessed the potential of these tea polyphenol-derived CDs to induce cytotoxic effects through the generation of ROS upon light irradiation [157]. The in vitro component of the study involved subjecting cancer cell lines (4 T1 cancer cell line) to the treatment with tea polyphenol-derived CDs followed by light exposure (white LED, 12 min), demonstrating the CDs' capability to selectively induce cytotoxicity in cancer cells while sparing healthy cells through the generation of ROS, leading to apoptotic and necrotic cell death in cancer cells [157]. This selective cytotoxicity is essential for minimizing damage to surrounding healthy tissue and enhancing the specificity of cancer treatment. Subsequently, the positive outcomes in the in vivo experiments using 4 T1 cancer cells in Female Bagg Albino (BALB/c) mice animal models suggested that the CDs are capable of effectively inducing tumor regression or inhibition through PDT while demonstrating minimal toxicity to healthy tissues [157]. By harnessing the efficacy of CDs in PDT, this study substantiated the prospect of tea polyphenol-derived CDs as a future strategy within the PDT landscape, aligning with the emerging trend of sustainable and environmentally friendly medical approaches. Another pertinent study conducted by Zhang et al. [158] adopted a holistic approach, encompassing both in vitro and in vivo evaluations of CDs in mitochondrion-targeted PDT. This comprehensive investigation ingeniously engineered CDs synthesized from Hypercium perforatum extract to possess tunable properties for generating ROS upon light activation, which is critical for inducing phototoxic effects in cancer cells. The in vitro experiments employing 4 T1 cancer cell lines provided valuable insights into the mechanism of action, demonstrating the ability of these CDs to localize within mitochondria and generate ROS, thereby disrupting mitochondrial function and triggering cell death specifically in cancer cells (Zhang et al., 2023). Importantly, the versatility of CDs as PS was further highlighted through their ability to induce both type I and type II PDT reactions, amplifying their potential for cancer cell elimination (Zhang et al., 2023). Moving beyond the laboratory, the in vivo studies conducted using BALB/c nude mice bearing 4 T1 tumor cell model further underscore the translational prospects of this approach, as CDs exhibit remarkable tumor growth inhibition and suppression effects (Zhang et al., 2023). Moreover, the focus on mitochondrion-targeted PDT added an innovative dimension to the study, as the specific targeting of this organelle enhances the selectivity and efficacy of the treatment while minimizing off-target effects. Thus, the study underscored the versatility of CDs, presenting a novel strategy that holds the potential for addressing limitations associated with conventional cancer treatments.

Moreover, doping CDs with specific elements has emerged as a transformative strategy to enhance their optical properties and stability, unlocking a range of pharmacological applications, especially in the realm of PDT for cancer treatment. Various in vitro studies had been proven the introduction of elements such as sulfur, copper, manganese, and others enhance the generation of ROS, initiating a cascade of events that lead to the inhibition of cancer cells. One compelling example is the research conducted by Li et al. [159], exploring the pharmacological role of sulfur-doped CDs (S-CDs) in enhancing PDT. The incorporation of sulfur doping not only enhances the optical properties of CDs but also play a crucial role in modulating cellular signalling pathways [159]. In vitro studies demonstrated the efficient generation of ROS by S-CDs under light irradiation, which is a pivotal mechanism in PDT. S-CDs exhibit a remarkable ability to inhibit the PI3K/Akt signalling pathway and promote p38/JNK signalling pathway, leading to the inhibition cancer cell survival and the induction of apoptosis, thus enhancing the therapeutic effects of PDT [159]. The irradiation of UV LED lead to an increase in the ROS levels which further induce oxidative stress in glioblastoma (U87-MG) and melanoma (A375) cancer cells, leading to apoptosis, whereas modulation of P13K/Akt and p38/JNK signalling pathway enhanced the therapeutic efficiency of PDT against both cancer cells types [159]. The specific targeting of signalling pathways emphasizes the precision of S-CDs in cancer treatment. Meanwhile, Zhang et al. [160] focused on manganese-doped CDs (Mn-CDs) for synergistic PDT of oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC-9). This study addressed the challenge of hypoxia in cancer therapy by demonstrating that Mn-CDs efficiently alleviate hypoxia, enhancing the overall PDT efficiency [160]. In vitro studies highlighted the ability of Mn-CDs to generate 1O2, a crucial mediator in PDT. Upon light irradiation, the manganese doping imparts catalase (CAT)-like activity to CDs, facilitating the decomposition of acidic H2O2 in situ to produce O2. This dual functionality contributed. To alleviating hypoxia and enhancing the PDT efficiency against OSCC-9 cells under 635 nm irradiation, showcasing the multifaceted potential of doped CDs in cancer therapy [160]. Moreover, Li et al. [161] also contributed valuable insights into the application of S-CDs as a highly efficient nano-PDT agent against OSCC (UM1 cell line). In vitro studies demonstrated that S-CDs induce a high rate of 1O2 generation under UV LED irradiation, resulting in acute stress response and Ca2+ influx in UM1 cells. This cascade events leads to the overexpression of caspase-3 and Bax proteins and the down-regulation of Bcl-2 protein, emphasizing the cytotoxic effects induced by sulfur doping [161]. The study underscored the potential of S-CDs as promising nano-PS with high efficiency against specific cancer types, contributing to the precision medicine approach. Furthermore, the work by Wang et al. [162] explored the optical and therapeutic properties of copper doped CDs (Cu-CDs). This investigation reveals the dual functionality of Cu-CDs in optical bioimaging and PDT [162]. In vitro studies showcased the efficient generation of ROS (specifically 1O2) upon light exposure, leading to oxidative stress and subsequent cell death [162]. The unique optical properties of Cu-CDs make them effective agents for bioimaging, while their ability to induce ROS generation contributes to robust therapeutic efficacy.

Similarity, Miao et al. [163] also provided evidence on the utilization of Nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) in PDT. The in vitro investigations focused on the synthesis and characterization of N-CDs and their subsequent application in photodynamic antibacterial and anticancer activities. By exploiting the unique ultralong-lifetime room-temperature phosphorescence (URTTP) property of N-CDs, the study established a distinctive characteristic that allowed for prolonged light emission upon excitation [163]. This property becomes a crucial factor in enhancing the photodynamic effects of N-CDs, leading to efficient 1O2 generation and subsequent antibacterial and anticancer effects [163]. The in vitro experiments involving bacterial cultures and cancer cell lines underscored the remarkable potential of N-CDs in inducing cytotoxicity and inhibiting microbial growth through photodynamic processes [163]. Moving into the in vivo realm, Miao et al. [163] advanced their investigation by employing animal models to validate the photodynamic antibacterial and anticancer effects of N-CDs within a physiological context. The intravenous administration of N-CDs in mice followed by laser irradiation demonstrated the successful accumulation of N-CDs within target tissues and the subsequent activation of photodynamic reactions [163]. The in vivo studies provided evidence of effective antibacterial and anticancer outcomes, indicating the potential of N-CDs for clinical PDT applications. The combination of the URTTP property and photodynamic effects showcased the versatility of N-CDs as promising candidates for precision therapeutic interventions. The unique URTTP property served as a key characteristic that enhances the efficacy of photodynamic processes, both in vitro and in vivo. Accordingly, the study's findings underlined the significant role of N-CDs as PS capable of inducing cytotoxicity and microbial inhibition through the generation of ROS. This multifunctional approach, which addressed both antibacterial and anticancer aspects, showcased the versatility and translational potential of N-CDs in the realm of PDT. Collectively, these studies comprehensively evaluated CDs as PS in PDT, paving the way for future advancements in precision cancer therapies. The incorporation of specific elements, such as sulfur, manganese, and copper, enhances the optical and therapeutic properties of CDs, making them promising agents for precise and effective PDT. The modes of action involve the generation of ROS, modulation of signalling pathways, and mitigation of hypoxia, collectively contributing to the targeted cytotoxic effects on cancer cells. The evidence from in vitro and in vivo studies underscores the potential of doped CDs as innovative and effective tools for advancing precision cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo application of carbon dots in combination photodynamic therapy
ce:section.ce:para: The integration of PDT with complementary therapeutic modalities has emerged as a promising strategy to bolster the effectiveness of cancer treatment. CDs, with their distinctive optical and photothermal properties, have garnered substantial attention as versatile nanomaterials for enhancing PDT through combination strategies, demonstrating considerable potential across both in vitro and in vivo contexts. Notably, the work of Song et al. [164] stands out, as they ingeniously designed a novel nano platform by synthesizing red light-emitting CDs (R@CDs) with efficient photothermal conversion capabilities. This platform was seamlessly integrated with the PS Ce6 via amide reaction, resulting in the creation of the R@CDs-Ce6 platform. A pivotal advantage of this system lied in its ability to address the challenge of poor PS dispersibility [164]. By leveraging R@CDs as carriers, the dispersion of Ce6 was substantially improved, thereby amplifying the overall effectiveness of PDT. This integration of R@CDs with Ce6 created a dynamic tandem capable of ameliorating the drawbacks associated with poor PS dispersibility [164]. Experiment findings from both in vitro and three-dimensional multicellular spheroids (3D-MCS) studies underscored the enhanced therapeutic potential of R@CDs-Ce6 [164]. Furthermore, the study’s in vivo experiment utilizing tumor mouse model provided compelling evidence of the effectiveness of R@CDs-Ce6 in inhibiting tumor growth [164]. The superiority of fluorescence imaging capabilities exhibited by R@CDs-Ce6 and its consequential wider tumor distribution further reinforced its potential as a transformative tool in cancer therapy [164]. Thus, this study showcased the promise of R@CDs in addressing limitations associated with PS delivery, potentially revolutionizing the landscape of cancer therapy by offering a safer and more effective alternative.

Furthermore, an innovative approach presented by Li et al. [18] involved the synthesis of a CQD-based nanocarrier system for combined chemotherapy and PDT. The system involved the conjugation of CQD and 5-aminolevulinic acid (5-ALA) with a mono-(5-BOC-protected-glutamine-6-deoxy)b-cyclodextrin (CQD-Glu-b-CD) moiety, followed by the loading of the chemotherapy drug DOX into the 5-ALA-CQD-Glu-b-CD system [18]. The resulting nanocarrier exhibited promising physicochemical properties, with high encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity for DOX [18]. In vitro studies revealed enhanced cytotoxicity and cell apoptosis upon treatment with DOX/5-ALA-CQD-Glu-b-CD, particularly when coupled with laser irradiation-induced ROS generation [18]. These results indicated the potential of a multifunctional therapeutic system capable of inducing synergistic chemo/photodynamic effects on cancer therapy. Moreover, Ren et al. [128] also shed light on the synergistic potential of CDs when combined with chemotherapy drugs for PDT. In this investigation, a groundbreaking theranostic nano-assembly drug was conceived, featuring CDs loaded with the PS Ce6, and the cisplatin chemotherapy prodrug Pt (IV). Through meticulous in vitro experiments using human esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (KYSE-150 cells), the researchers unveiled the promising prospects of GCDs-Ce6/Pt-EGF nano-assembly drug when administered alongside laser irradiation [128]. The intervention yielded remarkable improvements in the therapeutic outcomes, prominently manifesting through the generation of ROS, a key process implicated in inducing cell death [128]. Moreover, the translational potential of this innovative GCDs-Ce6/Pt-ECF nano platform, characterized by its excellent biosafety profile, was further underscored through in vivo experiments involving tumor-bearing mice models [128]. These investigations revealed a notable augmentation in tumor growth inhibition, particularly among tumors expressing the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) upon laser irradiation [128]. This effect surpassed the outcomes achieved by single treatments involving Ce6 and GCDs-Ce6-EGF, attesting to the enhanced therapeutic potential of this nano platform in combination therapy scenarios [128]. Mechanistically, CDs can act as both drug carriers and PS, allowing for targeted drug delivery and efficient PDT, resulting in improved therapeutic outcomes.

In addition, the integration of immunotherapy with PDT represents a promising avenue for enhancing antitumor immune response. Notably, Zhang et al. [19] have made substantial contributions in demonstrating the synergistic effect of CDs-mediated PDT and immunotherapy, both in vitro and in vivo. In this study, the synthesis of aggregation-induced emission and two-photon fluorescence imaging capability in the form of orange CDs (OCDs) using citric acid and (1R,2S)-2-amino-1,2-diphenylethan-1-ol showcases an impressive achievement [19]. This synthesis imparted unique properties to the resulting OCDs, enabling them to act as versatile PS, making them suitable for both PTT and Type 1 PDT [19]. The choice of utilizing 808 nm light for PTT and white light for Type I PDT showcased the versatility of OCDs in mediating distinct therapeutic mechanisms [19]. This versatility was pivotal for tailoring treatment strategies to target various aspects of tumor biology and microenvironment. [19] By capitalizing on the unique properties of OCDs, the study opened new possibilities for designing effective multimodal therapies that can be fine-tuned to specific tumor types and conditions [19]. One of the key contributions of this study lied in its immunotherapy potential. The transformation of protumoral M2 macrophages into antitumoral M1 macrophages through the generation of •OH during Type I PDT highlights an innovative mechanism for immune modulation [19]. This immunotherapeutic ability, integrated with the photothermal and photodynamic effects of OCDs, forms a synergistic trimodal therapy [19]. By combining these modalities, the study underscored the potent anticancer efficacy achievable through such a comprehensive approach. Additionally, the in vivo experiments involving tumor-bearing mice provided compelling evidence of the therapeutic efficacy and safety of OCDs-based multimodal therapy [19]. The observed inhibition of tumor growth and prolonged survival of mice validated the translational potential of this approach [19]. These results were crucial for establishing the clinical applicability of OCDs-based treatment and provide a strong foundation for further research and development in the field of cancer therapy.

In summary, the combination of PDT with complementary therapeutic strategies, such as chemotherapy and immunotherapy, holds great promise for enhancing antitumor responses. The studies discussed here demonstrate the potential of utilizing CDs in combination therapies to address the challenges of cancer treatment and offer innovative solutions for more effective and targeted interventions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo studies of carbon dots in photothermal therapy
ce:section.ce:para: CDs, a class of nanomaterials, have garnered significant attention for their unique optical properties and their potential applications in various fields, including biomedical research. In recent years, CDs have emerged as promising candidates for PTT, a non-invasive therapeutic approach that involves the conversion of light into heat to selectively ablate cancer cells [5]. In particular, their unique photothermal properties have led to extensive investigations both in vitro and in vivo, aiming to harness their capabilities for effective PTT against cancer.

One notable study by Lan et al. [168] exemplified the comprehensive exploration of CDs in the context of both in vitro and in vivo studies for PTT. The study effectively demonstrated the multifunctional capabilities of CDs in the realm of cancer theranostic, showcasing their potential as versatile agents for both imaging and therapy [168]. In vitro studies conducted in this research provided valuable insights into the photothermal properties of CDs. By employing HeLa cells as a model, the researchers conducted experiments to evaluate the photothermal conversion efficiency of CDs upon NIR irradiation (635 nm, 10 mins) [168]. The results revealed efficient cellular uptake of CDs and subsequent photothermal ablation of HeLa cells, demonstrating their potential for targeted cancer treatment [168]. Moreover, the study also harnessed the inherent fluorescence of CDs to enable two-photon-excited fluorescence imaging, enabling real-time monitoring of the PTT process within cells [168]. The in vivo component of the study further solidified the therapeutic potential of CDs in PTT. By utilizing a murine tumor model, the researchers investigated the feasibility of CDs for in vivo photothermal ablation. The results showcased the successful inhibition of tumor growth upon NIR irradiation (880 nm, 500 mW, 10 mins), validating the efficacy of CDs as PA in a complex biological context [168]. Furthermore, the study demonstrated the compatibility of CDs with in vivo imaging applications, highlighting their potential for guiding and monitoring therapeutic interventions [168]. Accordingly, the integration of fluorescence imaging and photothermal therapy, as exemplified by CDs, opens doors for precise and real-time monitoring of therapeutic outcomes. The successful demonstration of in vivo tumor growth inhibition also reaffirmed the promise of CDs-based PTT as a viable strategy for cancer treatment.

Furthermore, Geng et al. [169] provided valuable insights into the utilization of CDs for PTT against cancer. This study exemplified the significance of in vitro investigations in elucidating the photothermal effects of CDs. By synthesizing NIR-responsive CDs, the researchers demonstrated their capacity to efficiently convert NIR light into heat, leading to cancer cell ablation at remarkably low power densities [169]. This in vitro approach elucidated the potential of CDs as efficient PA even under mild irradiation conditions (808 nm NIR laser, 0.8 W cm−2), which could contribute to reduced collateral damage to healthy tissues [169]. Transitioning to in vivo studies, the findings obtained highlight the crucial role of CDs in achieving effective PTT within a biological context [169]. By utilizing a mouse model, the study underscored the practical relevance of CDs-mediated PTT for cancer treatment [169]. The administration of CDs and subsequent NIR irradiation led to a substantial reduction in tumor growth, offering evidence of the translational potential of CDs-based PTT strategies [169].

Another notable study by Kim et al. [170] also underscored the significance of designing CDs with enhanced NIR absorbance for efficacious cancer treatment. In vitro investigations conducted in this research shed light on the photothermal properties of CDs derived from Camellia japonica [170]. By utilizing cultured cancer cells, such as MCF-7 cells, as a model, the study effectively demonstrated the cellular uptake and localization of these bioinspired CDs [170]. The photothermal conversion efficiency of the CDs under NIR laser irradiation was examined, revealing their remarkable ability to convert NIR light into heat [170]. This in vitro evidence validates the potential of CDs as efficient PA, paving the way for further exploration in more complex biological settings. Additionally, the researchers also emphasized on in vivo studies are crucial for gauging the translational potential of CDs in PTT. The study employed a murine tumor model to evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of bioinspired CDs in vivo [170]. NIR laser irradiation was applied to the tumor site post-intravenous administration of CDs, resulting in effective photothermal ablation of the tumor [170]. The findings highlight the bioinspired CDs' ability to accumulate at the tumor site and trigger localized hyperthermia, leading to tumor growth inhibition.

A recent study from Jiang et al. [171] also presented a comprehensive investigation into the application of CDs derived from indocyanine green (ICG) for PTT, elucidating their potential through both in vitro and in vivo studies. The in vitro segment of the study involved the synthesis and characterization of ICG-derived CDs (ICGCDs), followed by a thorough assessment of their photothermal properties [171]. Through cellular experiments using cancer cell lines such as HeLa and A549, the study demonstrated the cellular uptake of these CDs and their efficient conversion of NIR light into heat [171]. The analysis of temperature changes upon laser irradiation substantiated the photothermal conversion efficiency of ICGCDs, showcasing their capability to induce hyperthermia within cancer cells [171]. These in vitro findings underscored the potential of ICGCDs as promising PA, setting the stage for more complex investigations. Transitioning to the in vivo context, the researchers leveraged animal models to evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of ICGCDs in PTT. Tumor-bearing mice were intravenously administered with ICGCDs, and subsequent NIR laser irradiation was employed to trigger photothermal ablation of the tumor [171]. The in vivo results showcased the accumulation of ICGCDs within the tumor tissue and the subsequent elevation of temperature upon laser exposure, leading to effective tumor growth inhibition [171]. The controlled and localized nature of this photothermal effect underscores the potential of ICGCDs as a targeted therapeutic strategy for cancer treatment [171].

The paradigm shift in cancer treatment comes with the application of doped CDs in PTT. The incorporation of specific elements, such as gadolinium and graphite nitrogen, not only enhances the optical properties of CDs but also imbues them with multifunctional capabilities. One compelling study by Mauro et al. [172] explores the utilization of Gd-CDs as potential tools for multimodal image-guided PTT and tumor monitoring. In this research, the gadolinium doping not only imparts enhanced optical properties to the CDs but also displaying pH-dependent behaviour, demonstrated the potential for multimodal imaging, allowing for precise monitoring of the therapeutic process [172]. The in vitro studies combined the benefits of PTT with real-time imaging capabilities, providing a comprehensive approach to cancer treatment. The studies demonstrated the concentration-dependent photothermal conversion ability of Gd-CDs in the NIR region, involves the efficient conversion of absorbed light energy into heat (significant increase in temperature observed), leading to localized hyperthermia and subsequently inhibited cancer cell proliferation and induced apoptosis [172]. Similarly, another study by Jiao et al. [173] explored the potential of Gd-Cds with AS1411 aptamers for targeted fluorescence and magnetic resonance (MR) imaging-guided cancer phototherapy. Both in vitro and in vivo studies reveal the proficient photothermal conversion of Gd-CDs under NIR irradiation, leading to a rise in temperature suitable for PTT [173]. Importantly, the incorporation of gadolinium enables Gd-Cds to serve as theranostic agents for MR imaging, offering a dual-modality approach for cancer diagnosis and therapy [173]. The targeted theranostic capabilities of Gd-RCDs are further validated through in vivo experiments, underscoring their potential in advancing cancer phototherapy. Furthermore, the work by Geng et al. [174] explores the enrichment of graphitic N dopants in CDs, showcasing their efficacy in mediating photothermal conversion in the near-infrared II (NIR-II) window with high efficiency. This study introduces a novel dimension to PTT by operating in the NIR-II window (wavelength: 1064 nm), which offers enhanced deep tissue penetration [174]. This further proven by their in vitro and in vivo studies whereby superior photothermal performance of N-CDs had been observed under NR-II irradiation, further showcasing the potential of enriched graphitic N-CDs for deep tissue penetration [174]. The enriched graphitic N dopants play a pivotal role in facilitating efficient photothermal conversion, contributing to the elevated temperature necessary for effective PTT. The enhanced stability and performance of N-CDs in the NIR-II window position them as promising candidates for advancing photothermal cancer therapy.

In conclusion, in vitro and in vivo studies have provided compelling evidence for the efficacy of CDs and doped CDs in PTT. These studies highlight various strategies to enhance the photothermal properties of CDs and their effectiveness in inhibiting tumor growth and inducing cell death. The localized hyperthermia generated by the both CDs and doped-CDs upon exposure to light results in thermal ablation of cancer cells, highlighting the potential of doped CDs as effective agents for PTT. Additionally, the integration of imaging capabilities, bioinspired synthesis, and dye-derived approaches contribute to the versatility and translational potential of CDs-based PTT. As this field continues to evolve, the insights garnered from these studies pave the way for the clinical translation of CDs-based PTT, providing a promising avenue for advancing cancer treatment strategies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo application of carbon dots in combination photothermal therapy
ce:section.ce:para: The integration of CDs into PTT and complementary therapeutic modalities is a burgeoning frontier with transformative implications for cancer treatment. A multitude of research papers have exemplified this concept, underscoring the versatility and potential of CDs to revolutionize cancer therapy through synergistic approaches. In one notable study, Lu et al. [16] accentuated the potency of CDs in bolstering cancer therapeutics. This study showcased a synergistic approach by designing a multifunctional hollow carbon nano platform coated with polydopamine and functionalized with CDs [16]. The in vitro experiments underscored the versatility of this platform, as it demonstrated enhanced cellular uptake and efficient delivery of chemotherapeutic drugs (DOX) [16]. The CDs' photothermal activity, when coupled with NIR irradiation, induced controlled hyperthermia, further augmenting the cytotoxic effects of chemotherapy [16]. Importantly, this study extended its investigation to an in vivo setting, where the nano platform exhibited targeted accumulation at the tumor site, enabling real-time fluorescence imaging [16]. The subsequent PTT resulted in significant tumor regression, highlighting the potential of CDs to amplify therapeutic outcomes through their photothermal properties [16]. Overall, the reported study exemplified how CDs can be effectively integrated into a multifunctional platform to enhance the efficacy of PTT while synergistically augmenting traditional chemotherapy, exemplifying a promising avenue for comprehensive cancer treatment strategies.

Furthermore, Khakbaz et al. [175] showcased the potential of CDs’ in sensitizing therapeutic carriers. The study capitalized on the unique properties of CDs by sensitizing lecithin-containing thermo-sensitive niosomes to create a novel platform for breast cancer treatment [175]. In vitro experiments demonstrated the successful incorporation of CDs, which significantly enhanced drug release from the niosomes upon NIR irradiation, leading to augmented cytotoxicity against breast cancer cells [175]. The CDs' photothermal effects further contributed to the platform's efficacy by inducing localized hyperthermia [175]. Moving into an in vivo context, the study validated the translational potential of this approach. The niosomes, sensitized by CDs, exhibited effective tumor targeting and subsequent NIR-induced PTT that resulted in remarkable tumor growth inhibition [175]. This study exemplified how CDs can be ingeniously integrated into a therapeutic platform (niosomal system), enhancing both drug delivery and photothermal effects, and thereby paving the way for an innovative and comprehensive chemo-photothermal approach for breast cancer treatment.

From a broader perspective, Qian et al. [17] underscored the potential of CDs in synergistic immunotherapy. The study introduced a novel concept of debris-mediated photothermal synergistic immunotherapy, where CNDs were ingeniously incorporated into a mesoporous silica framework [17]. The in vitro experiments demonstrated that the framework exhibited excellent biodegradability and controlled photothermal effects upon NIR irradiation, leading to efficient cancer cell ablation [17]. The CDs played a crucial role in sensitizing the framework to induce immunogenic cell debris upon PTT, thereby enhancing the antitumor immune response [17]. Moving into an in vivo context, the study substantiated the translational potential of this approach. The treated mice exhibited remarkable tumor growth inhibition, attributing the synergistic therapeutic outcomes to the orchestrated interplay between PTT and immunotherapy [17]. Accordingly, the reported study served as a striking testament to the versatility of CDs in facilitating a novel and multifaceted therapeutic strategy, underscoring their potential in advancing cancer treatment paradigms through the integration of PTT with immunotherapeutic approaches.

Moreover, the application of doped CDs in combination with PTT also showed their potential synergistic therapeutic effects. For instance, Jiang et al. [176] presented an innovative approach to address the challenges of triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC). Through meticulous in vitro and in vivo experimentation, the study presented a multifunctional approach wherein Gd-Cds were ingeniously designed to combine NIR-laser-triggered PTT with drug delivery for synergistic therapeutic outcomes [176]. The in vitro experiments showcased the remarkable photothermal conversion efficiency of the Gd@CDs, leading to effective cancer cell ablation upon NIR laser irradiation [176]. Importantly, the Gd@CDs served as a dual-functional platform by concurrently delivering chemotherapeutic agents (DOX), demonstrating controlled drug release and enhanced cytotoxicity against cancer cells [176]. The in vivo experiments further emphasized the translational potential of this approach. The targeted accumulation of Gd@CDs within tumor sites was confirmed through MRI, enabling real-time monitoring [176]. Subsequent NIR-laser-induced PTT resulted in significant tumor growth inhibition, demonstrating the powerful combination of photothermal effects and drug delivery [176]. This study exemplified how Gd@CDs can seamlessly integrate various modalities e.g. from imaging to therapy in order to achieve comprehensive and effective cancer treatment, substantiating their potential as a versatile tool in the field of oncology.

Collectively, these reported studies illuminated the remarkable potential of CDs in the integration of PTT with complementary therapeutic modalities. The studies collectively demonstrated how CDs' unique properties, including photothermal conversion efficiency, drug delivery capabilities, and immunomodulatory effects, can be harnessed to revolutionize cancer treatment paradigms. The synergistic outcomes observed in vitro and in vivo underscore the transformative impact of CDs in the realm of cancer therapeutics, fostering a new era of integrated and comprehensive treatment strategies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Future of carbon dots as anticancer agent in cancer treatment
ce:section.ce:para: The future of CDs as an anticancer agent is incredibly promising, offering a paradigm shift in cancer treatment through their unique properties and versatile applications. CDs, carbon-based, have garnered attention for their potential in various fields, including medicine, due to their remarkable tunable properties, ease of synthesis, and impressive biocompatibility [5]. The potential impact of CDs on cancer treatment is multi-faceted, encompassing their bioavailability, biocompatibility, role in synergistic treatments, targeting of tumors within internal organs, integration with AI and robotics, contributions to personalized medicine, and possible application in clinical setting.

The intrinsic versatility of CDs, coupled with innovative synthesis methodologies, underscores a remarkable bioavailability that paves the way for a transformative era in cancer therapy. By harnessing the potent properties of medicinal plants, natural products, and agricultural waste as precursor materials, researchers can unlock an eco-friendly, low-cost and sustainable avenue for CDs synthesis with advantages in biodegradability, biocompatibility, and availability [5,177]. This biogenic approach not only aligns with a growing emphasis on environmentally conscious practices but also offers an opportunity to imbue CDs with the inherent therapeutic attributes of these sources. Consequently, CDs synthesized from such origins hold the promise of synergizing the benefits of natural remedies with cutting-edge nanomedicine, amplifying their bioactivity, and enhancing their targeted potential as potent anticancer agents. Central to the future of CDs in cancer treatment is their innate biocompatibility, a cornerstone property that elevates them to the forefront of biomedical applications [5]. Their remarkable compatibility with biological systems establishes CDs as a safe and viable option for integration into innovative cancer treatment modalities. The minimal toxicity and reduced risk of adverse effects associated with CDs underscore their suitability for precision medicine approaches. Moreover, the ability to engineer CDs' surface characteristics and size allows for further customization, ensuring seamless integration with biological entities while minimizing potential drawbacks [31]. This intrinsic biocompatibility positions CDs as a powerful tool in the pursuit of safer, more effective, and patient-tailored cancer therapies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic treatment modalities and personalized medicine
ce:section.ce:para: The future of CDs as an anticancer agent is deeply intertwined with their potential for synergistic treatments and precise targeting of tumors, even within the confines of challenging internal organs. CDs exhibit a remarkable capacity to serve as versatile nano-carriers, ferrying therapeutic payloads such as chemotherapy drugs or targeted agents directly to cancer cells. This targeted approach minimizes collateral damage to healthy tissues, thereby mitigating the harsh side effects commonly associated with conventional treatments. Furthermore, CDs' optical properties can be harnessed for real-time diagnostic monitoring, enabling a comprehensive assessment of treatment responses and facilitating timely adjustments. The convergence of CDs with other innovative therapies, including PTT or immunotherapy, holds promise for creating powerful combination regimens that unleash synergistic effects, maximizing treatment outcomes. The trajectory of CDs in cancer treatment also poised to contribute to the evolution of personalized medicine. By tailoring CDs' properties to specific patient profiles or cancer types, clinicians can usher in a new era of tailored and patient-centric therapies. The advent of CDs-enabled theranostic, which seamlessly integrate therapy and diagnostics within a single platform, could usher in a paradigm shift in cancer management. This innovative approach enables real-time monitoring of treatment responses, allowing healthcare providers to dynamically adjust therapies based on individual patient needs, leading to more effective and adaptable treatment strategies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The integration of CDs with AI offer great potential for future cancer diagnostics and management
ce:section.ce:para: Intriguingly, the integration of CDs with the realms of AI, mathematical modelling and robotics is a revolutionary force that promises to reshape the future of cancer management. CDs, known for their superior biocompatibility and sensitivity, have the potential to serve as cancer biomarkers, offering the potential of early cancer detection, precise drug delivery, and personalization of the therapy. AI adds a revolutionary dimension to this strategy. Advances in AI and its potential contribution to nanomedicine offer a unique opportunity to realize its full potential in future cancer management, as AI has a tremendous ability to rapidly and accurately process and analyze vast medical datasets [178,179]. Through the effective learning capabilities of AI based on large databases, machine learning (ML) algorithms can be trained to interpret complex molecular profiling data generated by CDs during their interaction with cancer-related molecules [180]. AI-driven algorithms can recognize subtle pattern and anomalies, even in the early stages pf the disease, that may be missed by traditional diagnostics methods. This powerful combination enables not only more accurate and reliable cancer diagnosis, but also precise health monitoring, subtyping and staging, which is critical for customizing the most effective treatment plans [180,181]. For example, when CDs interact with cancer biomarkers, AI and mathematical modelling meticulously analyze the data. The potential interdisciplinary approaches to predictive biomarkers and AI is exemplified by research in which ML model have been used to identify predictive signals from multiple cancer biomarkers in peripheral blood samples and tumor biopsy materials, including analysis of whole-genomic profile, contributing to the selection of treatment strategies [180,182–184].

Furthermore, the potential therapeutic applications of AI extended to the design of AI-engineered CDs. These customized CDs, with their small size and surface functionalization potential, can enhance the precision and efficacy of drug delivery. In this context, AI-assisted analysis and pre-screening can helps identify target patients more quickly and accurately, facilitating the development of specific treatment plans and assignment of drugs or targets, laying the groundwork for nanomedicine design [181]. For example, AI can be used to optimize the drug formulations for CDs to ensure controlled release and stability, while AI algorithms are used to precisely target tumor sites for more effective treatments. In this realm, robotics system can play a key role in ensuring precise drug delivery and supporting minimally invasive surgical interventions. AI-driven combination therapies with CDs, loaded with multiple therapeutics agents, are also expected to revolutionize cancer treatment strategies in the future. Supported by mathematical modelling, AI can be combined with nanotechnologies to predicts the synergistic effects of combination therapies by helping to select drug combinations and determine the dose and frequency of administration of each drug [181]. These AI-driven strategies can rationalize factors such as drug targeting, distribution, chemical or physical properties of nanomedicines, and a patient’s dynamic response to the treatment, leading to the optimization of combination cancer therapy [181]. Adding robotics to this equation could further improve precision and minimally invasive interventions, thereby potentially improving patient prognosis.

In the field of AI-driven nanomedicine in the context of personalized drug targeting and delivery, AI can be trained to understand all aspects of nanoparticles’ behaviour, from drug loading and releasing to interaction with cell membrane, based on extensive database [181]. This understanding is crucial for creating efficient drug delivery systems, as AI can fine-tune the design of nanoparticles so that they can deliver drugs to specific target more efficiently. This, in turn, could lead to more precise and efficient cancer treatments, tailoring therapies to individual patients. In terms of the collaboration between AI and CDs, AI can be used to analyze a patient’s genomic and clinical profiles, enabling CDs to be design in specific way to deliver drugs with precision and in optimal doses, thus enhancing patient outcome. The synergy between AI, CDs, mathematical modelling, and robotics is laying the groundwork for a new era in cancer management, one that emphasizes improved patient outcomes, reduced side effects, and an enhanced understanding of cancer biology. This innovative integration is opening new prospects for precise, personalized and effective cancer treatment, with robotics playing a pivotal role in precision and minimally invasive interventions. It represents a transformative force with the potential to reshape the future cancer management.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of CDs in clinical settings and cancer management
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of CDs in clinical settings, particularly in the context of cancer management, has encountered various challenges despite their remarkable potential. CDs have demonstrated their potential in a variety of biomedical applications, including drug delivery, bioimaging, and therapeutic diagnostics, but their transition from research laboratories to clinical settings faces several obstacles [5]. While CDs have shown promise in preclinical and early-stage studies, the journey from academic research to clinical practice has been slow and complex. One of the key challenges is the need for extensive preclinical and clinical studies to evaluate their safety and efficacy. While CDs have shown biocompatibility in many in vitro and in vivo studies, rigorous assessment of their long-term safety in human remains a priority. Additionally, the regulatory approval process, particularly those of organizations such as the Food and Drug Administration (FDA), requires comprehensive data on the pharmacokinetics, toxicity and potential adverse effects of CDs. This process requires substantial time and resources as it involves multiple phases of clinical trials to ensure the safety and efficacy of CDs.

Numerous research studies have delved into the potential of CDs in clinical applications. Due to their good dispersibility, NIR absorption and photoluminescence capabilities, ability to bind to target units, good chemical stability and photostability, CDs have been investigated as imaging agents for use in cancer diagnosis [185]. Studies demonstrating the application of CDs in in vitro and in vivo bioimaging have emphasized their potential in clinical diagnosis. For instance, a study conducted by Huang et al. [186] explored the versatile application of CQDs synthesized from by-products of biorefinery process in both in vitro and in vivo bioimaging. The synthesized CQDs with excellent biocompatibility were used in vitro to visualize the cellular structure of SP2/0 murine myeloma cells line. In addition, their in vivo studies revealed that most of the CDs could aggregated and fluoresce strongly at tumor sites in nude mouse, suggesting that the as-synthesized CQDs are a viable bioimaging agent [186]. Due to the penetrability and good biocompatibility of CQDs, this study highlighted the potential of CDs in developing effective clinical diagnostics. Similarly, Hailing et al. [187] emphasized the application of CDs as a drug delivery systems in cancer therapy. In this study, DOX-loaded CDs passivated by PEI, were developed as an efficient delivery platform [187]. These CDs offer several advantages, including their fluorescent properties for imaging and their ability to encapsulate and deliver therapeutic agents (DOX) to cancer cells, which can significantly impact cancer therapy by enhancing the precision of targeted drug delivery [187].

Despite these promising studies, the practical application of CDs in clinical settings faces barriers such as standardization, scalability, and quality control. Producing CDs with consistent properties and quality suitable for clinical use is a considerable challenge. Therefore, ensuring reproducibility and quality control of different batches of CDs is a key issue, and establishing regulatory approvals and guidelines is necessary for safe clinical use. Moreover, standardized synthesis methods and characterization techniques for CDs are essential to meet the stringent requirements of regulatory agencies such as FDA. Achieving precise and efficient drug delivery to tumor sites (the primary goal of CD-based therapies) remains challenging and requires optimization of factors such as circulation time, drug release, and accumulation in the target tissue. In addition, establishing the clinical efficacy of CD-based therapies and diagnostic reagents requires rigorous and resource-intensive clinical trials to generate sufficient evidence of improved patient outcomes.

Despite these challenges, there are promising advances that offer hope for future clinical application of CDs. Collaborations between academia, pharmaceutical companies, and government agencies, as well as advances in nanomedicine and AI, can facilitate the translation of CDs into clinical applications. Financial support and grants can assist researchers overcome regulatory and financial barriers. Advances in the broader field of nanomedicine, including innovations in drug delivery systems, imaging technologies, and cancer therapies, could create opportunities for CDs to play a more significant role in clinical practice. Multifunctional CDs for diagnostic and therapeutic purposes are in development, with the potential to simplify the regulatory pathway and enhancing their clinical utility. As precision medicine continues to evolve, CDs can be tailored to individual patient profiles and disease characteristics, thereby enhancing their clinical relevance. The integration of CDs with AI can accelerate the disease biomarkers identification, drug design, drug delivery and treatment personalization, potentially addressing some of the challenges in clinical translation.

While regulatory complexity and safety concerns have marked their journey, the potential for FDA approval is promising. CDs possess a unique combination of properties, including biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence, and drug-carrying capacity, making them invaluable tools for personalized medicine and precise drug delivery. As research continues to reveal the full potential of CDs and address safety and quality concerns, their approval for clinical use, particularly in cancer diagnosis and treatment, becomes increasingly likely.

Collectively, the journey of CDs from the laboratory to clinical settings is fraught with regulatory complexities, safety concerns, and scale-up challenges. Nevertheless, the promise of nanomedicine, coupled with collaborative efforts and technological advances, offers hope for the future. With ongoing research and development, CDs have the potential to revolutionize cancer diagnostics, drug delivery, and treatment, ultimately benefiting patients and healthcare systems. While the path to clinical integration may be challenging, it is well underway, and the future holds promise for CDs in cancer management and beyond. Overcoming current challenges and capitalizing on promising developments are critical steps in bringing CDs from the research bench to the patient's bedside, where they can make a substantial impact on cancer care.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In summation, this review paper has illuminated the multifaceted potential of CDs as a future paradigm in the realm of anticancer therapy. Commencing with an insightful overview encompassing the historical trajectory, diverse synthesis methodologies, and characterization of CDs, it provided a strong foundation for understanding their burgeoning role in biomedical applications. The investigation into CDs' cytotoxicity profile for anticancer therapy, as showcased through a series of in vitro and in vivo experiments, underscores their capacity to selectively target specific cancer cell lines while exhibiting minimal impact on healthy normal cells. Furthermore, the adept biocompatibility of CDs has been demonstrated to synergistically amplify anticancer effects when combined with diverse treatment modalities such as chemotherapy, targeted therapy, radiotherapy, and immunotherapy. The application of CDs in photo-induced treatments, as both PS in PDT and PA in PTT, has been unequivocally substantiated through a range of meticulous in vitro and in vivo studies. Moreover, the transformative potential of CDs in the domain of combination therapy had further been underscored, wherein their integration with chemotherapy and immunotherapy, alongside their multifunctional application in phototherapy, had shown remarkable promise. Notably, CDs' dual roles as PS in PDT and PA in PTT have been illustrated as potent tools for effectively combating cancer cells. As we peer into the future, the outlook for CDs as an anticancer agent remains incredibly promising. Their potential to address critical aspects such as bioavailability and biocompatibility, coupled with the augmentation of synergistic anticancer treatments and collaboration with cutting-edge technologies like AI and robotics for precision diagnosis and treatment, heralds a transformative era in the evolution of personalized medicine. The integration of CDs into the clinical landscape is a tantalizing prospect, poised to reshape cancer treatment paradigms and foster a new dawn of tailored and efficacious therapeutic strategies. In essence, it casts a bright light on the significant strides that CDs are poised to make as a future anticancer agent, fostering optimism for enhanced patient care and improved outcomes in the complex battle against cancer.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Miss Hui Jing Hong embarked on her academic journey by obtaining her bachelor’s degree from Monash University Malaysia in 2020. Currently, she is studying for her PhD’s degree at the Institute for Research in Molecular Medicine (INFORMM), Universiti Sains Malaysia. Hui Jing’s research is at the forefront of innovation in the field of nanomedicine. Her research is specifically focused on the environmentally sustainable production of carbon dots sourced from natural materials. In addition, she is dedicated to elucidating the unique physiochemical characteristics of these carbon dots and exploring their potential applications in the realm of cancer research.

Dr. Ali Abdullah Shati received his PhD in 2007 from Aberdeen University, United Kingdom. He currently works as a professor in the Faculty of Science, Biology Department, Abha 9004, Saudi Arabia. His major research interests include molecular biotechnology, cytotoxicity of Nanomaterials, the therapeutic and protective effect of different bio-extracts and new formulations.

Dr. Mohammad Y. Alfaifi received his PhD degree in 2009 from King Saudi University. He currently works as a professor in the Biology Department of the Faculty of Science at King Khalid University in Saudi Arabia. His primary research interests include cytotoxicity and biological activities of natural products and green biosynthesized nanomaterials in biological systems.

Dr. Serag Eldin I. Elbehairi received his PhD degree from Ph.D. in Tissue Culture from Al-Azhar University, Egypt. He currently works as Associate Professor in the King Khalid University, Faculty of Science, Biology Department, Abha 9004, Saudi Arabia and Research & development manager in Cell Culture Lab, Egyptian Organization for Biological Products and Vaccines (VACSERA Holding Company), 51 Wezaret El-Zeraa St., Agouza, Giza, Egypt. His major research interests include cell culture, flowcytometry technology, co-culture system, cancer biology, cell spheroid, 3D culture technology, natural extracts, and pharmacological studies on natural products.

Dr Sreenivasan Sasidharan obtained his Ph. D. in 2007 from Universiti Sains Malaysia, Malaysia. He currently works as Associate Professor in Institute for Research in Molecular Medicine, Universiti Sains Malaysia. His major research interests include anticancer activity of medicinal plants and green nanoparticle.
